description:
It's the year 3001, the habitat of the Penguins reduced during the global warming and became very small. The Penguins evolved to less friendly animals and became truculent. Now every piece of ice is important to survive. Several clans fight for there lives. This game is played with two of those clans.

The aim of this game is to survive with as many penguins as possible, attack other penguins and nourishing your Inuit family.

Each player draws a card out a line of three open cards and that card indicates the actions you are allowed to perform. There are three types of actions. Lay down your tiles, move your penguins and attack other penguins. When you fight, you role dice!!
There are 10 types of tiles, most of them with a special effect to be carried out. So be careful where you place your tiles, 
it could harm yourself!

Meanwhile you have to support your Inuit family, by slaughtering killer whales, seals and opponent penguins. Every fight will be rewarded whether it is successful or not. It's your effort that is rewarded.

Each player has a character deck of cards and 3 lives (3 heart tokens). Players simultaneously flip their cards face up the fastest as possible in order to find a special Tech. There are some symbols printed on each Tech cards.

Players go on flipping cards up until they gather all the symbols in a specific order. The first player who does that Klashes his opponents with his Tech and a new round starts.

Most of the Tech cards have special powers that trigger when the Tech is achieved. In addition, each Tech grants points and the first player who scores 10 points wins the game.

If a player fails to achieve his Tech (i. e. they flipped the wrong symbol), he loses 1 life (heart token). When a player has no more life, he's out of the game.

This card game might be the fastest card game of the world!

Prosperity: Planning Your Future looks like Monopoly on the surface, but is a much more sophisticated game intended to teach children the realities of earning a living and keeping the family solvent and safe. Each player starts by picking a profession at random, with salaries ranging from 120 pounds a month for a postman to 300 pounds a month for an actor.

Player tokens then proceed around the board with opportunities to make investments in either unit trusts (which pay triple at game end) or a house, to use hire-purchase to buy household appliances, to put money into or take it out of banks and building societies (paying 8%) or Local Council (10%), as well as to encounter Accidents or Unexpected Expenses and to buy a wide variety of Insurance Policies against such ills.

Purchasing a property requires a minimum deposit, surveyors fee and solicitors fee, as well as monthly repayments of about 1% of the mortgage amount. Passing Start provides monthly salary for the occupation less all monthly payments (rent or mortgage, insurance, hire-purchase). The winner is the first to accumulate total assets of 15,000 pounds.

This made-in-England game is undated, but the graphics point to late 1950s or early 1960s (the family father on the box is smoking a cigar and wearing a jacket with cravat). The box claims a copyright, but there is no indication of the copyright owner or publisher, with the possible exception of a small cathedral-like logo on all four sides of the box. The rules and play suggest it might have been produced by a company in the investment or insurance business.

The Squaremino board game is a challenging and addictive abstract game for 2-4 players that uses a unique set of domino-like tiles. Players take turns adding sets and sequences to a public network of tiles, attempting to be the first to play all of their tiles.

Players of various skill levels can all enjoy this game as more casual gamers will enjoy a relaxing social experience and more advanced players will enjoy exploring complex strategies. The game play is a blend of competition and cooperation as players pursue individual goals while jointly building upon a common structure.

The generously-sized game tiles feel delightful to the touch and are pleasing to the ear as they are shuffled together. As the game play progresses, beautiful patterns of colors emerge, creating an elegant work of art by game's end.

South Africa is a land with a rich history, demanding terrain and spectacular resources. 1859 stretches from Cape Town through the Orange Free State to Johannesburg and beyond to Rhodesia. Tokens are non-blocking but do extract a cost for passage. The later discovery of Diamond mines in Kimberley affect the play, as does the even later discovery of extensive Gold deposits in the Transvaal. The game requires the usage of the hex track tiles and play money found in Avalon-Hill's 1830. New Little Companies are provided. 1859 lasts from 2-4 hours and is for 3-5 players. This is not a game for beginners. Most money wins the game.

ZORP is a shoot-em-up strategy game that combines fast-paced action with chess-like strategic maneuvering and decision-making. It's easy to learn and has both recommended and randomized board arrangements.

One player is a zombie master who controls a steadily growing horde of zombies. Every other player is a human who has been infected by the zombie virus. Humans have nine turns to get to a cure. Along the way they will gather weapons and events to help them survive.

In a two/three player game, there is only one cure on the board and in a four player game there are two. This means that in a three/four person game, one human is always guaranteed to not get a cure. Humans will want to work together, at least somewhat in order to overcome the odds, but in the end, one person cannot be rescued, so humans may turn against each other to slow each other down.

Each human begins with one random weapon and their turns consist of two movement phases and an attack phase, which they can use in any order. (Move/Move/Attack, Attack/Move/Move, Move/Attack/Move) A movement is one tile in a non-diagonal direction and an attack lets you use all of the ammo at your disposal during the attack phase. Each weapon has different properties and the ammo is reloaded each turn.

When a human lands on a weapon tile, they add it to their inventory and are able to duel wield weapons. If they land on an event, they trigger it immediately. Some events persist throughout the game (like finding fast shoes, etc.) some are one time use (like finding a live grenade, etc.).

The Zombie Master's turn begins by moving all of the zombies on the board. Different zombie types have different movement abilities. After moving existing zombies, he/she spawns new zombies to add to the board. Certain turns will also trigger a zombie event after the new zombies have been placed on the board. Zombie events are generally good for the Zombie Master, but expert humans can find ways to exploit certain zombie events.

The players take turns until either all the cures have been acquired, or time runs out and none of the turns have been acquired or if the zombie master can zombify all the humans in the game.

Description from the designer:

BaRPG is the unholy result of a one-night stand between a drinking game and  card based RPGs. From the side of RPG, BaRPG has inherited a melange of exciting characters to play, a definitely sexy level system, dice-rolling and booty (Err… I mean epic loot!). From drinking games, it has inherited a series of ridiculous games that will keep you drinking. Though in the end there will be a clear winner, we like to think the true prize is in the friends you will make along the way…

The game is played between 3 to 7 players. Each player receives a character card, and a level and damage tracking card. Every round all players (except whoever last levelled up) roll a dice, highest roll then checks if what was rolled is higher than their current level. If so, that player levels up, reads out their character's power (which is a drinking game), and the group then plays that game. At the end of the game, someone will have to take 'drinks' as well as take damage. The game then enters a market round, where players can 'buy' additional item cards. These item cards can be used at any time during the game, and can either assist players or thwart the efforts of others. The game then returns to a new round of dice rolling.

The first player to reach level 6 wins, and yes there is even an item that can be played against another player to put him/her back to level 1. There are in total 10 characters with associated games, and these characters will swap during the game as part of the item card mechanics. Additionally, losing a total of 4 'sober' (damage) points will also return the player to level 1, force them to change their character, and lose all accumulated items.

Wakkawakka is a turnbased two Player game. The goal is to capture as many cherries (victory points) as possible. Therefore players move their adventurer token in a pacman styled labyrinth. 
But just collecting cherries would be to easy. That's why each player also has a hunter to stop the enemy adventurer. Hunters cannot collect cherries, but when they capture the enemies adventurer it is reset to its starting position.

So players have to decide how to spend their 3 move actions each turn on their hunter or adventurer token.

The first player to collect 11 points wins the game.

From the publisher:

Crisis is a modern game with many innovations like a virtual, generic map, no general certificate or money limits, rounds instead of operating rounds and stock rounds, structure purchases, and signals but without some traditional elements like off-board connections, terrain difficulties, small cities, empty big cities, or privates. This gives the game a new feeling already on the surface. Square tiles are used to simulate subways in a downtown megalopolis.

While once 1830 could be called the premier game of stock manipulation and 1841 is the game with the greatest speed of technological development, now Crisis is the toughest, most complex, and most unpredictable game of business strategy. Measuring complexity is hard but years of testing have produced counter-strategies for every strategy and not enabled me to foresee just one round, what is possible in 1830 or 1841.

So what makes Crisis so tough? First of all, companies can purchase unlimited numbers of foreign shares. This creates complex combines. Second, companies are forced to own more and more foreign shares, what makes good shares scarce. This is an extra duty besides buying a train. Third, foreign assets may only be bought from the or as a director. Fourth, all shareholders can be responsible for under-endowed companies while some might avoid supports and let the director lose his shares even though having supported. Fifth, other shares in such companies may not be given away and can cause more supports. Sixth, half the companies have only certificates that already allow a director change. Seventh, trading many old or new certificates quickly restructures combines, depletes companies, or inflates money. Eighth, the stock market and the round order, which involves players and companies, are particularly flexible. To conclude, dividends are paid only to owned foreign shares.

While the game has a balanced start and good route construction remains important, it becomes more and more essential to excel at wheeling and dealing. Clearly, Crisis is the game of devious business manipulations.

The first historical Platoon Commander module delves into the dynamic early days of the Korean War. The North Korean People's Army (NKPA) is relentlessly marching south, and the United States Army, vastly outnumbered, finds itself fighting a series of desperate battles in an attempt to buy enough time for the Pusan Perimeter to be finished. Chaffees and T-34s, Skyraiders and guerilla attacks; it's all here.

Platoon Commander: Korea 1950 features four tense scenarios:

Task Force Smith: The NKPA armor shrug off everything the Americans threw at them. Can they survive?

Taejon: The 21st Infantry Regiment & 78th Heavy Tank are all that stands between the NKPA and the unfinished Pusan Perimeter.

Chochiwon: The 21st Infantry Regiment faces ten-to-one odds. But US airpower might even the score.

Task Force Lynch: The daring Pusan Perimeter Breakout.

The objective of the game is to be the first to empty their hands.
In turns, players attempt to play a card by matching the previous card color, number or symbol. If unable to play, players draw a card from the draw pile, and if still unable to play, they pass their turn. Wild and special cards spice things up a bit:

     Card "Skip turn"

boardgame: Roman Italy (fan expansion for Ticket to Ride)
description:
This follows the principles of the basic Ticket to Ride game on a special board with special cards and rules. The object of the game is to gain the most points by travelling various routes and outwitting other players. You will need the train pieces and the train cards from either the US or Europe base games (or you can buy them from the Days of Wonder shop.

There is an extra set of Tourist cards each with a value to be earned by visiting that city. These cards are dealt/picked up alongwith tickets in the same quantity and with the same 'keep/dispose' rules. There are multifunctional God cards too. There are also up to 9 bonuses for the end-of-game scoring.

The board does not have any grey tracks or parallel tracks - but you can move forward 2000 years and time-travel to Rotterdam by the Gotthard Base tunnel for extra points.

There are many new options in the rules: exchange all your cards at the start but keep all the new ones: discard 5 cards and pick up 5 new ones, as your turn: and many more. You can also call time when your wagon stock reaches 3, if you want, giving you the advantage of stopping other players completing tickets.

Adapt is a highly strategic survival card game where you take on not only the environment, but your closest friends. Players take turns facing challenges on the island, exploring, hunting, and deciding when to betray the other players all while they are hunting down the precious relics to win the game. You can avoid dangerous misadventures by collecting and holding onto the right supplies, like the fanny pack!

At the beginning of each player's turn, they must draw an action card that they must play..the island gives them no choice! Players then take turns rolling a 4-sided die for the actions they can perform. During their turn they can choose from 5 different actions: Gathering, Exploring, Stealing, Attacking, or playing a card's action from their hand! To perform these actions, the player must roll the 20-sided die and get a 10 or higher (for attacking, gathering, or exploring) to be successful, or a 15 or higher for stealing! As the game progresses, the players must collect all three relics and keep hold of all 3 to win! It's either holding the relics, or be the last survivor standing.

In Shadow Strike, you take control of a small group of ninja warriors; trying to be the last ninjas standing on the map. Shadow Strike mainly focuses on movement tactics on a hexagon board, as well as dice rolling and "take that" card play. Once only your ninjas are on the board, you win!

description:
Sand and Snow is an expansion that will enhance your Mistfall and Heart of the Mists experience with a set of 110 brand new cards, you can add to any of one of the sets, or use when you mix them.

What is Sand and Snow?

Let’s start with a new type of Enemy: the Lieutenant. Easily recognizable by lacking a Resolve value, a Lieutenant is an alternate version of an existing Enemy which, when eliminated, allows you to take a Reward card right out of the display. Most Enemies appearing in Mistfall and Heart of the Mists will see their Lieutenant version in the Sand and Snow expansion.

New opportunities to receive Reward cards call for (you guessed it!) more Rewards. And you will get a nice big cache of brand new General Rewards. I’ve noticed over the last year that many of Mistfall fans wanted more cool loot, and besides bringing some to the Heart of the Mists box, you can now get an even greater number of new weapons, armour and arcane items to use against the Mists.

Sand and Snow will also feature a few new Allies, and a set of Special Event cards, drawn at specific times during the game, becoming essentially more permanent versions of Encounters. If you can beat them within a few turns of the game, you might be rewarded for your effort, and if you decide to ignore them, they will come back to bite you!

This game is about a horse race from Berlin to Viena and vice versa in 1892. One space represents 10 Kilometers. Movement is determined by a 18-sided die.  The die results are as follows:
blank side: 1 space forward (rider leads his horse on foot, 6x)
Schritt:    2 spaces forward (pace, 2x)
Trab:       3 spaces forward (trot, 2x)
Galopp:     4 spaces forward (2x)
Carrieré:   5 spaces forward (1x)
Verirrt:    1 space backward (lost, 1x)
Gestürzt:   stay (fallen, 1x)
Rast:       stay and pass a turn (rest, 2x)
Lahm:       drop out of the game or start again at space 1 (lame, 1x)

The game is probably from the 1890s.

This 1890 game by Edgar O. Clark is part of the publisher's Tokalon series and should not be confused with the 1900 Parker Brothers game The Rough Riders, which cashed in on Teddy Roosevelt's famous charge up San Juan Hill. Edgar O. Clark describes its game in the rules as simply "illustrating the wild life in the far West".

It is a simple spin and move game, with a few special spaces (Charge, Run Away, Ditch, Halt, Thrown and Wire Fence) that provide pluses or minuses. A piece that lands on another swaps places (sending the other back), and the four corner circles are safety points where bumping is not allowed. First player back to the start wins.

Lis: Gra Karciana is a card game created based on the comic book "Lis" published by Wydawnictwo Sol Invictus. Its action takes place in the today Poland and derive from such genres as criminal, thriller or superhero.

The are 66 cards in the deck, which will cause the gameplay to be full of twists and turns. On each of them there are illustrations kept in the style of the comic book "Lis". In the "Lis: Gra Karciana" players will be able to play as one of four comic book characters, which must collect the appropriate goals.

There are different type of cards: character, goal and action cards. Each player receives 1 character card face down, 1 goal card and 3 action cards. All remaining cards are mixed together and shuffled. In your turn you can do 2 moves from the following ones: draw a card, play an action card, discard 2 cards from your hand to draw 1 card from the discard pile. The card hand limit is 7 cards.

At any moment if another player is playing an action card, you can block its effect by saying "stop" and discarding one goal card from your hand. Other players can block your "stop" by doing the same.

You win immediately if at any point you manage to collect 4 out of 5 goal cards matching (giving 3 points) your character. If nobody was able to collect 4 dedicated goal cards, the game ends after the turn of the player which drew the last card from the deck. In this case the players will sum the points gained from the goal cards based on the scoring from their character cards. The player with the biggest score wins.

Brothers! Salvation is at hand! King Rat is holed up at the  top  of  the  grandfather  clock.  If  we  can  negotiate our way safely through the clock and plant 4 sticks of dynamite  directly  beneath  his  lair  we  can  end  his  evil reign  once  and  for  all.  Move  swiftly  but  softly,  mice. Our future is in your hands...'

Dynamice is a free to download print and play game. It is a nine  card  nanogame  of  guiding  dynamite-carrying mice  through  the  insides  of  a  grandfather  clock.  Can you guide all 4 mice to their target before any of them hit an obstacle and explode?

description: This is a pack of twelve alternate Character cards that were given away to backers of the Exposed Kickstarter campaign. They are labeled Item #37 through Item #48.

Space Connection is a two-player deduction game. Each player has a two-part board, with one part being used to set up their "planets" and the other for the opponent's attempts to find them.

Each player has twenty plastic planet pegs which need to be placed in exactly nine groups of contiguous pegs (abstractly representing the Solar System's planets). Each player also has pegs representing lasers and reconnaissance ships of different strengths.

The ships can be placed to investigate one space, one space plus the six adjacent spaces, or all spaces in one of the triangles the board is divided into. A ship is only returned if its space actually contains a planet peg, otherwise it remains on the opponent's board and cannot be used again. The lasers are only useful when at least two have been placed in a line, as they reveal planets in all spaces between them.

The first player to find all twenty opposing pegs is the winner.

boardgame: Warhammer Quest: The Adventure Card Game – Witch Hunter Expansion Pack
description:

“The test is simple: if he does not burn, he is a witch. If he does burn, then he was an innocent.”

Witch Hunters will travel far and wide to hunt down and destroy the forces of evil. Their pursuit is utterly relentless, and they will do whatever they must in order to eliminate the evils they encounter—even if that means they suffer a few innocent casualties.

The Witch Hunter Expansion Pack allows you to enjoy your Old World dungeon adventures as one of these grim and resolved holy warriors. The savage Dwarf fighters known as Troll Hunters seek glorious death in battle to make up for some great shame in their past. As a result, they routinely seek combat with the most dangerous monsters they can find.

There's nothing subtle about the Troll Slayer Expansion Pack for Warhammer Quest: The Adventure Card Game. It's just a giant heap of fearless, axe-wielding, battlecry-screaming, death-dealing Dwarf. The Troll Slayer's three hero cards, eight actions, and three legendary gear cards are all aimed squarely at leaping into the thick of combat, unarmored, and cutting down your foes.

Pokemon XY Mega Evolution - ポケモンXYメガシンカボードゲーム is a board game from Takara Tomy. The players uses a spinner that gives players move points. A player then move his/her a creature on the board and read the text on the spot they land on, or move a hero to get upgrades. There are also different paths to take. First hero to the goal is the winner.

HELLO TAIWAN is a card game about  KUSO(funny) Taiwan's native affairs. There are so many dumbfounding things, complex issues of history and ethnic groups in Taiwan. 
We carefully designed for each character's appearance , skills and features in the game to transform serious social events into funny cardset ability. 
Let's play the game with smile and enjoy it !

[Component]
Game BoardX1
CardsX200 (include Minions and Spells)

[How to play]
1. Shuffle the cards and deal 30 cards to each players as their own deck.
2. Decide which player will go first randomly. 
3. Each player draw 4 random cards from their own deck.
4. Each players have their turns alternately. At the start of each playes's turn one card is drawn from their deck and added to their hand. At the first turn of each players, they have 1 Skill Point (SP) ,then at the second turn of each players, they have 2 Skill Point (SP) ,and so on.
5.During your turn, you may play any of your cards, including summoning minions, casting spells. Playing a card will consume the amount of SP stated in the top-left corner of the card. You are also able to command your minions to attack opponent's minions or player.

[Minions] 
Minions remain on the board after entering play and only leave when destroyed. Minions may not attack on the turn they enter play, but may attack each turn afterward.

[Spells]
Spells are cards which change the state of play, dealing damage, healing wounds, or buffing Minions.

[Winning the game]
A player wins the game when he reduces opponent's player to zero life.

哈囉臺灣是一款本土的KUSO時事卡牌遊戲。 臺灣有太多讓人哭笑不得的事情，有太多歷史與族群等複雜的問題，在遊戲中，我們針對每­一位角色精心設計外型、技能及特性，讓嚴肅社會事件，轉化成有趣的牌組功能，就讓我們­滿懷笑容的參與，並且享受其中吧。

description:
German cycling game from the late 1920s published by a company from Leipzig.

There are six riders in the game, divided into two teams. Three of the riders (black, red and yellow) have a special lap counter in their back wheel. These riders are moved with the three coloured dice on the outer lane (with roman numerals) and try to win a lap over their competitors. The game rules calls them "Tempofahrer", which might be translated as "endurance riders".

The three other riders are sprinters. They race each other on the inner lane at given intervals and compete just for some points which count only in case of a tie in the number of laps won.

There are the three coloured dice.

These three dice are thrown in turns and the riders in the outer lane advance as many fields as dice mark their colour or their teammate's (three-coloured dice would have been enough and probably better). However, if in a turn none of the dice shows your colour(s), you fall back to the red square with no lap won.

So you hope for a long run of throws with at least one of your colours to win a lap. And most often than not you get a bad throw when you have completed more than three quarters of a lap and you are very near your objective. That hurts!

Meanwhile, the sprinters wait their turn to play. At intervals (the rules are not particularly precise here) the coloured dice are put aside, the riders on the outer lane stay wherever they are, and a short sprint takes place between the three riders on the inner lane. No complicated rules here either, just throw and move, with a couple of penalty squares (2 -only reachable by a double one on the first throw- and 13). Teams whose sprinters land twice on these squares during the game (in different sprints, most probably) are penalized with one lap.

The rules state that some player or onlooker can call a sprint anytime during the game for a special price he is willing to offer (a present for the winner of that sprint). Just like the "primes" in 6-day races.

The player whose rider(s) perform best during the race by crossing the finish line first wins the game.

German cycling racing game, the board game is two sided, one side takes place in Denmark though since it was published by a company that also rents holiday cottages in Denmark, the other side of the board represents a fishing trip in Norway. The B-side is played with pedestrians, not with cyclists.

The game comes with 4 card board cyclists and 4 card board pedestrians.

The game is won by the player whose figure crosses the finish line first. On side A of the board the players use cyclists and race to the finish line with a role & move mechanics, side B is similar but here the players use pedestrians.

This is a free print-and-play game published by the cycling lobby from Austria. 
It's about the Tour of Austira.
Strangely enough, the game has a French name. 
It features roll & move mechanics, is playable up to 4 and has a map with the Tour of Austria track printed on.

boardgame: Le Tour de France: La Boîte à Quiz
description:
Released by French company Marabout in their 'La Boîte à Quiz' series. This trivia style party game is about the Tour de France.

The author is French cycling journalist Jean-Paul Ollivier.

The yellow box contains 160 cards, divided in the five categories. The back of the cards is of a different colour for each category.

To give the game some "strategic" edge, there are 4 types of cards in each category.
22 (out of 32) cards in each category named after the category. Besides, there are 3 "booster" cards, 3 "accident" cards and 4 "contre la montre" (time trial) cards in each category. These cards have a different symbol in the back, making a total of 20 different card backs (5 colours, 4 symbols each).

The 22 "normal" cards give one point for a correct answer.The "booster" cards give three points, while the "accidents" deduct three points from the player in case of a wrong answer. The three categories of questions in the card are given different names for each type of card, which only adds a baroque touch to the game. In all cases the player only has to answer to one question, chosen by the opponent.

On the other hand, the time trial cards have four questions and the player must answer as many as he can in one minute, getting 1, 2, 3 or 5 points.
Winner is the player who scores the most points.

Trivia style card game endorsed by the Fédération Française de Cyclisme.
It was published by a French company from Toulouse.

Obscure self-produced French cycling sports game.

The game was made by two guys called Gérard and Patrick (that's where the GÉPAT brand comes from), and it was an ambitious project since it comes with 4 different boards. A Time trial board, a flat coarse and 2 mountain stages.

The game has 11 plastic cyclists (including one in the yellow & mountain jersey) and is playable up to 8 (with each players one cyclist) or less (for example with 2 players each one has a team of 4 riders).

Movement in this racing game is decided by playing out cards (so no dice).

You can play a 1 day race or a stage tour with a general classification using all 4 boards.

This game's box is an incredible German box-set that looks like a modernist piece of furniture. The entire box measures 22 x 17 x 12 cm. Not pocket-sized, but not too big either. There are 11 games inside, two in each drawer (with 2-sided, 4-folded game boards, except for one which contains the games: Dame, Mühle, and Puff (checkers, nine men's morris, and backgammon). There is a cycling board game, as well as a rowing and horse racing game.

The cycling board game is labeled "Radfahrt" on the drawer, but is actually called "Bicycleclub" in the rules.

The components / pieces are made from metal (cyclists, horses and row boats).

Up to 6 players can take part and they can bet on the outcome of the race(s). The board measures 40 x 20 cms and is just a 50-square-long track without any particular indication on it, except for the arrow that tells us that the riders will move clockwise. Both the cyclists depicted on the board and the tin figurines included in the game have an unmistakable 19th century look. However, while the riders on the board look like racing cyclists, the tin figurines rather look like tourists.

The rules are very simple. This is a betting game in which any number of players can take part. Players do not manage a particular rider but just bet on the outcome of the race. Players must agree upon the numbers of laps, and cyclists move according to the throw of dice. During their turn every player can move any cyclists since it is unknown who placed bets on which rider.

In a way this has similar mechanics like games as like Heimlich & Co. (hidden identity) and Reiner Knizia's classic horse racing game Winner's Circle (published by Alea) which also featured players making hidden bets during a race. After the race is over and the riders have finished the races, the players reveal their bets and he who placed the most accurate bets wins the game.

Cycling board game published in Germany (in 1926), it's about indoor cycling and takes place on an oval track, those races are know as 6-tage rennen since they last 6 days.
It has a roll & move mechanic but overtaking is only possible in straights (which have black dots) with a higher number, the overtaken rider moves to the neighboring white dot, misses a turn, and resumes the race from the black dot.
You must reach exactly the last black point before each curve and overtaking in the curves is not allowed. The rider behind will stay behind losing any extra pips.

The zombie apocalypse happened and the zombies won. Now what? There aren't any more people to eat and the rotting eternity is down-right boring. So, the zombies came up with the idea (if that's even possible) that foot-races would be neat. They certainly can be exciting – especially when pieces of the contestants fall off along the way!

Zombie Shamble is a fast-paced card game where players take on the roles of zombies trying to do their best to get across the finish line. It's not all that far away, but remember that you're an animated rotting corpse and all kind of craziness can happen in just a few steps.

First to 25 steps wins. Deal out 6 cards each, then draw a card, play a card. The cards are Steps (move towards finish line), Calamities (eg a leg falls off; fixed by Duct Tape card), Fortune (usually cancels a Calamity), a Trump card (eg Steely Eyed, so cannot be distracted). Oh, and that's exactly 25 steps, not more. Else the Zombie Judges won't be happy. But might be hungry.

description:
User summary
THEほうきギターズ (THE Houki Guitars) in English means "THE Broom & Guitars" (with the emphasis on THE intentional).

In the game of THE Broom & Guitars, you lead a music band of high-school students, and compete against rival bands with your live performance.

This game is based on the traditional Japanese game  Shichi Narabe with some twists.

There are four suits that correspond to parts of the band: Vocal, Guitar, Bass, Drums, and each suit has seven levels ranging from 1 to 7,

You buy an instrument card you want to upgrade, and at a later turn you place that card out at appropriate position on the board like Shichi Narabe (the card being placed must be adjacent to an existing card), but unlike Shichi Narabe, vertical connections and diagonal connections are allowed as well as horizontal connections. You advance your skill of that instrument (or vocal) in this way.

Once all cards of one suit are bought, it's time for live performance. All players count victory points according to skill levels they reached for each suit.

That's one round. After the second round, the highest scorer wins the game.

Publisher's summary
メンバーを集めてバンドを結成します。
バイトをして楽器を入手したり、楽器を練習をやりつつ
ライブで客をより熱狂させたほうが勝者です。
カードを一定のルールを元に出し、それに応じてコマを進めるゲームです。最も効率よくコマを進めることを目指します。

description: The Potions Expansion contains 20 new spell cards (five in each color) and 12 potion bottles.

"To the ramparts! Show no quarter! For none shall be given."

Grim Heroes is an unreleased fantasy dice game, originally scheduled to be the first game to utilise the DiceQuest game system used in the games, Ancient Terrible Things (2014), Konja (2017) and Ancient Terrible Things: Reawakened (2023). 
Grim Heroes remained in development until 2012, at which time the Dicequest game system was implemented in the game, Ancient Terrible Things. 
An advertising card featuring Grim Heroes is included in the first edition of Ancient Terrible Things (2013), with the intention of the game's eventual release after the publisher's second release, Snowblind: Race for Pole (2015).  Ultimately, Dicequest Version 2 was published as the 2-player-only game, Konja (2017), and Dicequest Version 3 in the game, Ancient Terrible Things:Reawakened (2023).

—description from the publisher

The expansion of the tribe goes on in Targi: Die Erweiterung, an expansion for Targi. With water as a new element, the tribe has more flexibility in how it will extend itself. More specifically, in the game water functions like a joker, e.g., you can swap two water tokens for any one goods token. Many of the new tribe cards, which replace the set in the base game, allow players to choose to spend gold or water tokens, which can make it a little easier to build.

The Targia — a Tuareg woman — is a special pawn like the robber, but has a more positive effect for players. She starts in the opposite direction of the robber and moves each turn one edge card. When you place one of your Targi on the same card, you receive a favor: Either take a goods token of your choice or spend one good to reveal the top card from the draw pile and receive what it shows. Now a less attractive edge card might be more interesting because the Targia offers an additional reward.

Finally, shifting sand cards give the players rather strong immediate advantages when they place a Targi on them — but since these shifting sands are placed next to the central playing area, you lose an intersection on the central cards.

Roosan gets another interesting ability in Catacombs: Teleport. It's the simple, standard teleport effect as an Ability, but it's one more run-around-like-a-chicken-with-its-head-cut-off thing that Roosan can do in each fight. This promotional card will be included in the Catacombs & Castles Kickstarter package, but also was offered at Origins 2016, so fans [of Roosan] could start using it immediately.

Additionally, according to the publisher's website, The Shadowlithe Fist (for the Thief) and the Teleport Chicken promo cards are included in the Catacombs: Wyverns of Wylemuir expansion.

Description from the publisher:

Imperial Settlers: Aztecs bring new rules, a new resource, and a sixth faction to the world of Imperial Settlers!

Prayer actions and blessing tokens bring religion to the world, while the Aztecs build temples and organize festivals and ceremonies to gain favors and be blessed! With this expansion, all other factions will also be able to add religion and their own pantheon of gods to their Empires.

This expansion includes a new faction board, the Aztecs faction deck, blessing tokens, and expansion cards for the original factions that complement the Aztecs abilities.

description:
This is a simple roll and move game where each player must try to move five men "across the alps" on his playing card. Each card has a row of circles marked "5", two rows of mountain scenery, and a row of goats. Unusually, the rules are printed on the front cover of the box.

Players take turns rolling a single die. Rolls of 2, 4 and 6 count for nothing. A roll of 5 allows a man to be put on the first row. A roll of 3 allows a move directly from the first (5) row to the goat row. And a roll of 1 allows a man to move forward a single row - but once in the alps, a 3 roll is useless to him. First player to get all five men to the goat row wins.

In Wacky Pirates you are racing your Pirate Ship to the cursed Skull Island in search of a big treasure. But beware... there are other Wacky Pirates on the horizon! You will need to find Treasure Maps to sail faster, use Rum to re-roll your dice, and fire your Cannons to attack other players. Meanwhile the Skull Island will try to take your Silver Coins by cursing your dice. Do you have what it takes to become the richest Wacky Pirate? Grab your Pirate Dice and go on an adventure in this fast-paced racing game!

Wacky Pirates is a quick press-your-luck style racing game driven by 5 large custom wooden Pirate Dice. Each player is a Wacky Pirate with a unique ability. The goal of this game is to gain Silver Coins by cleverly performing actions on your Pirate Dice. The first player with 6 Gold Coins is the winner of the game!

You might consider this game as Big Bang Theory meeting Happy Families in the Roaring 20s - in Latin.

This is very much a Latin educational game, and consists of a deck of 65 cards divided into 13 "books" of five cards. As the rules put it: "Each book embraces declensions and conjugation forms ending in certain letters, as, es, i, um, etc."

A book's word ending appears in capitals in the middle of each card, followed by a specific example, such as "-US Gen. Sing. 4th Dec." (genitive singular fourth declension). Partial information to the other cards in the book appear on the four corners of a card along with one or more question marks.

Play is similar to Happy Families, with players taking turns asking other players for the cards needed to complete a book. But in this game, a player must ask for a card by its full form, by filling in the question marks on a card he already holds. And to ask for the King card in each book, the player must recite correctly all of the other forms in the book! The player to collect the most books wins.

Dumme Dame is an abstract 2 player game printed on a postcard. Only a pencil is needed (and an eraser for multiple plays).

The playing field consists of 4 x 4 spaces. One player starts and writes a number between 1 and 3 into one of the fields and adds an orthogonal or diagonal arrow to the field. The next player follows the arrow and starts his/her player on the target space (the written number is the distance in spaces from the origininal space). Some limitations apply: 

     No move may lead over or onto an already used field.
     No move may lead towards an arrow (no back and forth movement). 


The player who cannot make a legal move loses.

On Kickstarter January 16 2025. Pledge here. https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/markhwalker/stalingrad-eastern-front-1942-43

Old School Tactical:Stalingrad is an expansion to the Old School Tactical system featuring a large map, counters and a scenario book covering small unit actions during the battle for Stalingrad.  The map is an exact recreation of the Nail Factory area of the city.  The scenario book contains numerous scenarios pitting Soviet and German troops in close quarters battle.

One of the most famous battles of the Eastern Front. Not a day goes by at the Flying Pig Games Multiplex that we don't receive an email, tweet, snapchat, or carrier pigeon asking us to reprint the Stalingrad expansion for Old School Tactical VOL I. Well, we found a way to fit it into our schedule, so we're doing it!

description:
Cation is a drawless connection game for two players: Black and White. It is played on the intersections (points) of an initially empty square grid (board). The top and bottom edges of the board are colored black; the left and right edges are colored white.

A crosscut is a 2×2 area with two diagonally adjacent black stones and two diagonally adjacent white stones.

Black plays first, then turns alternate. On your turn, place a stone of your color on an empty point. If this completes one or more crosscuts, remove exactly one enemy stone from each crosscut. You cannot complete a crosscut if your opponent just placed a stone in it or completed a crosscut anywhere on the board.

Passing is not allowed, but, if you have no legal moves available, your turn is skipped.

You win if there is a chain of orthogonally connected stones of your color touching the two opposite board edges of your color.

To make the game fair, White will have the option, on their first turn only, to swap sides with Black instead of making a regular move.

Rhode is a drawless connection game for two players: Black and White. It's played on the intersections (points) of an initially empty square grid (board). The top and bottom edges of the board are colored black; the left and right edges are colored white.

				
				
					DefinitionsA weak pair is a set of two like-colored, diagonally adjacent stones such that there is no like-colored stone adjacent to both.

A crosscut is a 2x2 set of stones consisting of two diagonally adjacent black stones and two diagonally adjacent white stones. A stone can be part of several crosscuts at the same time.

				
				
					PlayBlack plays first, then turns alternate.

On your turn, if there are any friendly weak pairs on the board, place a stone of your color on an empty point that is adjacent to the two stones in one of those pairs. Otherwise, place a stone of your color on any empty point.

After your placement, remove all friendly stones, except the one just placed, that are part of any crosscuts.

You win by creating a chain of orthogonally connected stones of your color touching the two opposite board edges of your color.

To make the game fair, White will have the option, on their first turn only, to swap sides with Black instead of making a regular move.

--Introduction--

The 12 Towers is an RPG board game for 2-6 players. The objective is to take control of all. Active Towers, and/or eliminate the opposing players. 
Players take the role of “Keepers”, powerful rulers that control a Tower, and its surrounding territory. Throughout the course of the game, Towers will be 
gained, lost, or even destroyed. Controlling Towers will involve combat, diplomacy, and teamwork. However, it may also involve treachery and deception.
                 
            While the objectives of the game are clear, it is entirely up to the players to decide how they play the game individually. Some may be aggressive 
and charge head on, while others might focus on defensive actions. In the end, when players interact, they are in complete control of their own fate. 
Not only do you bring a strategy to the game, you bring your personality as well.

--Brief Overview of the game--

Players: 2-6
Game Type: PvP, RPG, Exploration, Board Control
Game Board: 32 x 32 Inch Dodecahedron (13 pieces)
Movement Style: Set space amount (Can be increased or decreased)

Game Components:
 - 12 Keeper Figurines
 - 12 Tower Figurines
 - 12 Colored Die
 - 576 Game Cards
 - 6 Tracker Sheets
 - 12 5x7 Tower Cards

Game Run Time:
 Easy/2 Players - 30/60 Minutes
 Medium/4 to 6 Players - 45/90 Minutes
 Hard/8 to 12 Players - 120/180 Minutes

--Game Board--

The board for The 12 Towers uses a hexagon battle grid. Players can explore, battle, cast spells, set up walls and defensive structures, and even set traps. 
There is no limitation to where you can go, or what you can do. It is important to know what the different colored spaces and regions do. 
Remember, the rules are for a standard game, and players have the freedom to alter these rules and regulations to better suit their needs.

--Towers--

Each Tower in the game has a unique theme, as well as it’s own strengths and weaknesses. Remember, it is important to explore options, and try out other Towers. 
You may have a favorite Tower, or discover a useful combination that can turn the tides of battle, and become an intimidating force!

Your Tower is the home of your Keeper, and it’s surrounding territories belong to you and your citizens. Whether you’re the Brutal Orc King of the Fire Tower, or the 
Undead Lord of the Black Tower, your Keeper is the King of it’s territory. As you play the game, you’ll gain control, or lose Towers. 
Be sure to use strategy and planning when you take over the board.

--Power--

Power is the heart of The 12 Towers. Power is a simplified number that represents everything that you are; Health, Mana, Currency, Influence, everything. 
Almost everything in the game effects Power. Whether you are taking damage from weapons, or casting spells. All players begin the game with the same amount of Power. 
The standard starting amount is 2000. This number is them divided between your Keeper, and your starting Tower. 
This number can be adjusted to use needs, or accommodate additional players.

--Cards--
    
 The core version of The 12 Towers contains 576 cards. There are 2 types of cards; Items, and Events. Items are cards you can equip, use, cast and break. 
Event cards are primarily used to give the players an advantage, as well as set the stage for role playing, and gives life and scenery to the game. 
Events are the main source of gaining more item cards.

boardgame: Star Wars: The Card Game – Meditation and Mastery
description:
From the publisher:

Throughout the Opposition cycle, the rivalries and feuds between affiliations dominate the game. The Jedi against the Sith. The Rebellion against the Galactic Empire. The galaxy’s scum and villainy against the loyal smugglers and spies. Now, with the third Force Pack in the cycle, you’ll have the chance to choose your side and battle to decide the fate of the galaxy.

Meditation and Mastery is the third Force Pack in the Opposition cycle for Star Wars: The Card Game, and this expansion offers ten new objective sets (two copies each of five distinct sets), granting new options to every affiliation. Here, you’ll find new affiliated fate cards for the Rebel Alliance and the Imperial Navy, alongside some of the other new fate cards introduced in this cycle. You’ll also gain the opportunity to fly and fight alongside recognizable characters from the Star Wars saga, including Wedge Antilles, Nien Nunb, and Admiral Screed.

Contains objective sets 241-245.

boardgame: Epic Card Game: Keira, Wolf Caller Promo Card
description:
The  box comes with 200 Epic Card Game card sleeves and 4 corrugated cardboard dividers, one for each faction, as well as a single promo card:

Keira, Wolf Caller
Wild Human Champion
Cost: 1
Ability: Ambush • Blitz Your wolf tokens have blitz, +1 attack and +1 defense. Tribute → Put two wolf tokens into play.

Manipulate the Universe using stars, planets, comets, supernovas, black holes and gravitational waves to impose other players !!!

Use Stars to win victory points or create your constellation.
Use Planets to bonus your Stars.
Use Comets to destroy Planets.
Use Supernova (massive explosions) to flip the adjacent tiles (which are not on their side of the Universe).
Use Black Holes to engulf and disappear adjacent tiles (which are not on their side of the Universe).
Used Gravitational Waves to alter the SPACE (move an adjacent tile to another place) or TIME (flip a tile for example, a Black Hole disappear or to make it appear a Star that was destroyed).

Game Turn:
In his turn a player can do one of the following:

     Steal tile (depending on the level of difficulty, store up to 2 in hand) and place it.
     Not steal or play tile and activate the effect of a tile panel (Gravitational Wave one, for example).
     Do not steal or play tile and activate GRAVITY (move any tile to an adjacent space)


Victory:
CONSTELLATION Game Mode (2-6 players):
The player who first represent their constellation on the board, wins.

UNIVERSE Mode game (2-4 players):
The player with most stars in his "universe", after playing all the tiles, win.

Cycling card game, the player who can deplete the other players deck wins. 
Each card features famous cycling champions including Laurent Fignon, Roger De Vlaeminck, Tom Simpson, Joop Zoetemelk, Eddy Merckx, Bernard Hinault, Fausto Coppi, Jacques Anquetil, Alfredo Binda, Eugène Christophe, Gino Bartali, Fabian Cancellara, Bradley Wiggins, Mark Cavendish, Cadel Evans, Erik Zabel or Marco Pantani.

Via hand management players have to beat the other players using skils from their champions like panache, climbing sprinting or stamina.

The player who can run the other player's deck down wins the game.

A Cyclo-Cross / mountain bike board game published in 2013 in the UK. 
There are two editions of the game, named "Slim" and "Pokket". The rules for the smaller "Pokket" game are a simplified version of the already simple rules for the bigger "Slim" game.

The game uses a roll & move mechanic as the players race over a track that features mountains, to reach the finish line first, he who crosses the line first wins.

Collect as many bottles as possible by winning Trickin’ Camel challenges and/or by catching the bottles he flings out of his bottle crate.

There are 3 types of challenges in this game. 

     Guessing game: the lights flash on the game base and players must remember the last light flashed - If the player chooses the correct light, the camel will make a victory noise and the player can take a bottle out of the crate otherwise camel spits a little bit water.
     Memory game: the lights flash on the game base and players must remember the sequence of flashing to earn a bottle.
     Speed game: Players must press the lights until the sequence stops to earn a bottle.


As soon all the 20 bottles are taken the player with the most bottles wins the game.

Kort på kanten ("Card on the edge") is a shameless party game in norwegian, where the purpose is to make funny, absurd and unfitting combinations of words, phrases, persons and situations.
To generalize, it's the norwegian version of Cards against humanity, with some new rules.

Play begins with a judge, choosing a red question or fill-in-the-blank card from the top of the deck and showing it to all players. Each player holds a hand of ten white answer cards at the beginning of each round, and passes a card (sometimes two) to the judge, face-down, representing their answer to the question on the card. The judge determines which answer card(s) are funniest in the context of the question or fill-in-the-blank card. The player who submitted the chosen card(s) is given the question card to represent an point, and then the player to the left of the new judge becomes the judge for the next round. Play continues until the players agree to stop, at which point the player with the most points (red cards) is the winner.

In addition there are some new rules:
"Bestemor" (gradma) - draw an extra white card from the draw pile. If that card wins, agree on a punishment.
"Sidesprang" (side step) - before you read the white cards, draw another red card and use that instead.
"Kapitalisme" (capitalism) - discard one of the red point cards you have won to "punish" other players or switch 3 white cards.
"Folkestyre" (democracy) - Judges are stupid! Everone can vote. The card with most votes win!
"Samspill" (Interaction) - Too many players? Team up with more people on each team.

From the publisher (translated):
"Kort på kanten is distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution-Noncommercial-Share Alike 4.0 license - that means you can use and remix the game for free, but you can't sell it..."

Burgers & Beasts is about building the best burger for the masses of adventuring customers that come your way. These adventurers will face many great foes, but their greatest challenge will be in finding a good meal.  That's where you come in.  Customers will be pouring out the Adventuring Cave, hungry for their favorite food: Burgers! Along with those Adventurers also come the fearsome Beasts that attack the nearby castle. Your job is to create the best burger to attract the strongest fighters and defeat the Beasts that threaten the castle…and gain a little treasure while you’re at it!

Build Burgers: Defeat Beasts; Gain Diamonds!

description:
Sea of Clouds: Kitty, Unicorn, Love Ray is a set of three promotional cards for Sea of Clouds.
Simply shuffle them with the other Loot cards after setup. Whenever one of them shows up on top of the draw pile, the game is momentarily interrupted. The active player picks the card, reads it aloud and immediately applies its effects. Then, the card is discarded and the game goes on normally.

description:
Vintage cycling board game made in China, it has a magnetic board and comes with 4 large 6cm plastic riders with a magnet on the bottom. The game works with a roll & move mechanic and a mechanism that allows you to select a gear that determines the speed of each cyclist.
Players put bets on each, after the race has finished bets are revealed and the player who put his bet on the winning cyclist wins.

From the publisher:

Our story begins with a rash promise made by the well in the castle courtyard. The princess was playing with her golden ball on the edge of the well when the ball fell into the water. The princess was sobbing… in her despair, she even promised the frog who hurried to help her that in exchange for the ball, he may go with her to the palace. 
The frog helped gladly, hoping that this girl would finally break the curse and turn him back into a handsome prince once again with her kiss, but when the princess held her favorite toy in her hands again, she didn’t want to keep any of her promises anymore, and she left him. The frog went after her to remind her of her forgotten promise…

User summary:
Game play:

     Each player plays with a frog figure.


Starting from the 4 corners of the maze , frogs advance on tiles that are laid face down. When your frog lands on a tile path is discovered. 
Make sure you avoid the maid though, she chases the frogs.
When a frog is caught by the maid, it has to start again from a corner of the maze and the path will be hidden again so memorizing what is on which tile is important.

     the  first player to reach the castle via the path (avoiding the maid) in the middle of the board may kiss the princess, the frog will become a prince now and that player wins the game.
family: Folk Tales & Fairy Tales: The Frog Prince

description:
From the publisher:
Every evening, Uncle Bob takes the two dachshund siblings, Spot and Louise, for a walk. While playing with her brother in the park Louise s ball rolls away. Louise runs after it, but she is so tiny that she gets lost among the bushes and objects of the park. When she realizes that she can not see Uncle Bob anymore, she starts to look for them right away.

In the meantime Spot also starts searching for Louise. Which doggie will find the other? A strategy game for children who love to play hide and seek.

User summary
Game play:

     The goal is to find the opponent's dog (pawn) before it finds you.
         Pawns (dogs) moving on a grid of 5 × 5 squares of 7 × 7 squares depending on the selected difficulty level. Each row and each row contains a decorative element: a leaf, a kite, etc.
    



         On your turn, you roll two dice. The pink dice shows how many questions (1 to 3) you can ask. 
    


The questions are of the "Looking to .... what do you see? ". 
We can look to the city (north), the park (south), River (west) or the road (east). With luck, the answers may give the exact location. Often uncertainty remains.

You can then move the maximum number of spaces shown on the black die (1, 2, 3, 3, 4 or 4) to try to reach the opponent's piece.

In this game of hide & seek the player who finds the dog of the other player before his dog is found wins!

From the publisher:
In Bananas there are cards showing monkeys in three different positions, and banana cards. When it is your turn, you can lay down one, two or three cards to line up three monkey cards. At the end of the game the winner is not the player with the most monkeys, but the one with the most bananas!

User Summary:

Game play:

The 60 cards Bananas are divided into 24 banana cards and 36 Monkey cards. The goal is to recover as many bananas as possible.

When playing cards, rows are formed under a stack of banana cards (banana cards or played in one of the banana pile stacks, the monkey cards in one of the rows), the , a player who can play three different monkey cards under a row wins all the banana cards in that row.
The player who has the most banana cards at the end og the game wins.

The princess is imprisoned in the castle. 
All the knights come rushing to save her…To earn a kiss from the princess they will have to: win a sword, trek over the mountain, collect the key to the keep, then cross the river.

(text from publisher's website)

Are you the knight that will save the princess? The first player who can overcome the obstacles and save the princess from the dungeon where she is kept captive wins the game.

How the game is played:

     each player (up to 4) has a wooden figure representing a knight, they start in the (4) corners of the board.
     the wooden figure of the princess, who is trapped in the dungeons of the castle, start in the middle of the board (castle)
     the two dragon figures start in the caves.
         during the game the players can gain swords (these are neede to defend against a dragon) or keys
         via dice rolls (the dice have no pips but images on them) the swords can be won
         in the caves the players can gain cards but beware of the dragons! These cards are needed to gain a key
         once they key is obtained the players still need to pass a bridge (a dice is rolled there) and when they do they can free the princess with their obtained key.
    
     the player who succeeds in releasing the princess from the castle wins the game

boardgame: Ophidian 2360: Survival of the Fittest – War Machine vs. Bio-Hazard
description:
Ophidian 2360: Survival of the Fittest is a non-collectible expansion to the Ophidian 2350 CCG arena combat game as well as a standalone game that pits futuristic gladiators against each other using unique and thematic mechanisms.

In the game, each player brings a team of up to four gladiators to the table, along with a customized deck that's driven by the selected gladiators. Gameplay revolves around a momentum-based system that allows the player with the momentum to continue to take actions until they lose momentum, at which point their opponent picks up and continues.

Players use resources to supply their gladiators with equipment and minions (support characters). The crowd gets involved through cheer and also provides an alternate victory condition. Win the crowd, and you win the game!

Mix and match cards from any Ophidian 2360: Survival of the Fittest decks:

     Ophidian 2360: Survival of the Fittest – Mental Block vs. Warrior's Resolve
     Ophidian 2360: Survival of the Fittest – War Machine vs. Bio-Hazard
     Ophidian 2360: Survival of the Fittest – The Art of War vs. Otherworld Allies


Playing with four players will require a total of four playable decks.

Many character options, 4 different win conditions, and a large number of in-game choices/options makes this a truly unique and engaging game!

Emoji are not new to this earth...

They have lived in an unseen parallel dimension for thousands of years engaged in fierce battles to find their one, true ruler. Now you can join the fight.

In Emoji Wars, players choose a commander, form an army of allies and use magic and weapons to battle their opponents for dominance over the Emoji empire.

When a player's commander has been killed, that player is out of the game. The player with the last commander standing wins Emoji Wars and rules the Emoji Empire... until the next battle!

The general idea is easy - Players pick a Commander, choose 3 Allies and then battle their opponents with magic spells and weapons. The numbers on the cards line up so you can see if a character has the ability to wield a particular spell or weapon. You can also see how many "Life Points" a character has or how much damage he has taken from a weapon or spell.

1. Card Types
Gold: Commander
Green: Ally
Blue: Spell
Red: Weapon
Yellow: Energy

2. Reading the Cards
Each card has information about the character, spell or weapon in the numbers below the image. A character card shows life points, magic or strength ability and the energy needed to launch an attack. Weapons and spells cards show how much ability is needed to use them and how much damage they will do to a character by reducing his life points.

3. What a Hand Looks Like
After a player has their "Army" assembled, they draw weapons and spells cards from the blue and red decks. Energy cards are included in these decks and get chosen randomly. A player's hand is visible to the rest of the table. Note that there are also "Shield" cards that add to a character's life points.

4. Attacks
On each turn a player can attack his opponents if they have the weapons and engergy needed to do so. As an example attack, Orin is using the Deep Freeze against Garlon. The spell card is placed under Orin to show that his life points have reduced by 30. On a later turn, the player draws a Shield, which he adds to Garlon to increase his life points to 30. This is how much damage it will now take to remove him from the game.

5. Win
When a player's Allies have been killed off, he fights with his Commander. If his Commander is killed he is out of the game. The last player with a standing Commander wins Emoji Wars.

In Stripes, players have a row of seven cards on the table featuring many horizontal lines in a variety of colors. The goal is to make a single stripe of one color across your card set. Each turn, you select one of the action cards to change your cards: flip, swap, steal, draw, etc. There are a certain set of actions available each round, depending on the number of players. How many moves will you take to complete your stripe and win?

boardgame: The Endless Land: The Endless Dwarven Land
description:
An expansion for The Endless Land, this brings the Dwarven realm into to the mix.  Adds the ability for up to 5 more players to join the game.

Includes 4 modular map boards, 5 characters, over 300 tokens, and over 30 quest cards.

boardgame: The Endless Land: The Endless Elvish Land
description:
This expansion for The Endless Land adds the expansive Elvish realm to the game.  This expansion adds up to 5 players to the game.

Includes 4 modular map boards, 5 characters, over 300 tokens, and over 30 quest cards.

From the publisher:

Hipsters and Hamsters is a light card game for individual or team play. To begin play, each player is dealt four cards. In a unique twist, players can win the game in one of two ways. Players win as a Hipster by having no cards in their hand at the end of their turn. Players win as a Hamster by having eight (or more) cards in their hand at the end of their turn.

There are four card types. Object cards serve as simple discards. No action is taken when Object cards are played. Action cards trigger the action shown on the card when played, helping players eliminate, or accumulate, cards. Calamity cards can be played at the start of an opponent’s turn, forcing the opponent to take the action on the Calamity card. Comeback cards can be played on Calamity cards, in which case it becomes the turn of the person playing the Comeback card. Depending on the Comeback card, that person either takes the action on the Calamity card, or ignores it, before starting their turn.

Despite its pick-up-and-play game mechanic, Hipsters and Hamsters is filled with dynamic and precisely-balanced strategy, as opposing sets of victory conditions cause players to flip between playing styles in reaction to draws and opponent actions, all while attempting to control turn order to alter the flow of play.

In the Basic game, players do not declare which character style (Hipster or Hamster) they are playing as and can switch between styles at any time, as many times as they wish, allowing them to adjust their strategy on-the-fly. In the Advanced variant and Team play, players declare their character style in advance and receive bonus points for winning the round as their declared character.

Ludothèque is a game of games collecting and club development. It uses Games Cards system. Players are owners of the game clubs trying to buy good games, organize game tables in order to acquire the most prestige points.

On your turn, you may (1) sell and buy games, and (2) add 1 game to make game table more attractive for gamers. New game on a table must has the same or wider player range, that a previous game's one. Game tables with 3 games are considered completed and give income to their owner.

Game table with easy games gives 1 prestige point, with medium weight - 2, and with heavy games - 3 prestige points. In order to win the game, you must reach 15 prestige points before your opponents do.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: Pop Culture Trivia party game is hours of trivia fun for fans of everything Pop Culture. From Marlon Brando to Miley Cyrus and Walkmans to iPods, this game is littered with the people, products, and trends that have shaped our world.

To win Super Predator, you must be the first player to host on your territory the super predator - lynx , bear, wolf or eagle - which will populate your area if all the pantry is present. So you have to plant trees and hedges, and maintain your rivers to accommodate wild animals and constitute the fauna - flora balance.

Winning condition:
The first player to have in his territory:

     in his forest: a deer, a doe & a boar
     in the plains: two partridges, a hare & a sparrow
     in the river: a duck, a pike & a carp



     If all of the above animals are present the conditions are ready to introduce a super predator (lynx, bear, wolf or eagle) in your territory, the first player to do this wins the game!


Gameplay:

     In the beginning of the game each player gets a pawn, 3 event cards and an intervention card.
     turns are played clockwise
     on his turn a player rolls the dice and moves his pawn the corresponding number of steps. If the pawn lands on a forrest spot he may either plant or remove a tree, on an agriculture spot he puts or removes a parcel, on a fauna spot he gains or removes an animal, if the pawn lands on an event spot he may play an event card.


The moment that a player reaches the winning conditions so that he can introduce a super predator, he wins the game.

In Riversea the players are participating in a fishing contest.

Gameplay:
The board features two tracks, on which your pawns can move. An external track (on the 4 sides of the board) for fishing in the river and an internal track (in the center part of the board) for fishing in open sea.

Each player starts with 15.000$ cash and 15 equipment cards. During the game the players can upgrade & buy new / better equipment.

The first player to accumulate 200.000$ value in money, fish & equipment wins the game.

The goal of this economic game is to get as rich as possible during the 10 rounds that the games last, so that you can afford yourself to buy a private island.

Gameplay:

     Each player starts with 100 million $ starting capital, a pawn and a personal player board in his color that states the possible actions.
     Each round consists of 5 phases: in phase 1 an event card is revealed, in phase 2 each player choose secretely in which market he is going to be active (this is revealed simultaneously), in phase 3 each player (in turn order) may choose an open card from the center of the board, in phase 4 player can buy in the markets via a  blind bidding mechanism, in phase 5 the player can sell to the markets.


The game ends after 10 rounds, he who accumulated the most money wins Crésus.

A simple Australian children's roll-and-move game produced around the time of the Second Anglo-Boer War (October 1899 - May 1902). Probably published just after Federation in 1900, the game celebrates Australia's new nationhood as well as its participation in the South African conflict, in which the various Australian Bushmen's Contingents played a major part. It follows the path of a typical Bushman, proceeding from his enlistment to training, embarkation, arrival in South Africa, and engagements with the enemy.

The game is basically a re-implementation of the traditional Game of goose, with players rolling dice and moving around a 60-space track. Most spaces have a themed text: Riding test, promoted to lieutenant, ordered to the front, taken prisoner, etc. Some of these spaces also incur bonuses or penalties (moving forward or back a number of spaces, to a particular space, or miss a turn).

The board includes coloured illustrations of bushmen rounding up cattle in the Australian outback prior to enlistment, a street parade of a Bushmen's Contingents, transport ship, field hospital, field prison, troop ship S.S. Medic, and battle scenes showing Australian prisoners guarded by Boers, and Australian troops attacking a Boer position.

Simple Australian roll-and-move race game around a 100-space oval horse race track. Unlike the real Melbourne Cup which is a straight race, the game features 4 jumps.

The board is monochrome, which suggests an early publication date. The race was first run in 1869 and annually since.

Card Swap is a 2-5 player, fast-paced game that challenges your memory and strategy skills against your opponents'. It's easy to learn for most ages and great for family game night!

Play to collect all four of the same card by swapping with cards around the table. Try to remember where cards have been moved to steal the win!

Simple roll-and-move race game, with a yacht race theme. The board consists of 75 numbered spaces. Players roll dice and move along the track (which looks like the tacking of a yacht) from the wharf to the winning post. Some spaces have bonuses and penalties.

Recorded in the National Archives of Australia as first registered and exhibited in 1912 by the National Game Company. In time a small board version with the same layout was produced by Westley Games

Close examination of the board game shows some obscure artistic freedom, with the actual date (1912) represented in the  small life ring on the gameboard along with the National Game Company trademark and "K.Y.C" (K? Yacht Club). It might be suggested that the K stands for the designers surname, Christopher George King.

The National Game Company produced another game simply titled "Yacht Race". This was considerably different in layout and had a 60 space path. It is therefore listed elsewhere.

Simple roll and move race game around a 60-space track, with a yachting theme. 12 of the 60 spaces offer bonuses or penalties. Unusually, each of the 4 players has his own path, parallel to each other, but separate.

The first version of the game Pre dates the National Games trademark. It appears Mr. W. Owen of Ballarat manufactured the Yacht Race game under his name prior to continuing production under the National label. This appears to date the first version somewhere between the late 1890's to early 1900's. Advertisements are seen as early as 1902 for the game.

As with many of the National Games produced, the game over the years appeared in three general forms; 
1. A large hardback board with a small separate box containing the playing pieces.
2. A small boxed version containing a thin carded board and pieces.
3. A large boxed version with a thin carded board (but larger in dimensions than the small box), playing pieces

The National Game Company also produced a similar nautical themed game called the "National Yacht Race" in 1912, but this game has 75 numbered spaces, a different layout and a different Registered number. It is therefore listed elsewhere.

Test your patience with this 2-6 player card drafting game.

Each player controls a scientist that has one thing on their mind... Irritating the other scientists! This frustratingly fun card game takes aim at the family audience, and is most fun with 6 players.

There are two decks (a community deck and an idea deck), and six 20 sided die that come in each game.

At the beginning of each game, a player receives ten cards. Each player will roll their die and whoever rolls the highest goes first. This will continue clockwise.

From the community deck players find resources to help them along with making their awesome ideas come to life!

To create an idea, each player will need 1 lab coat, 2 schematics, and 2 chemical sets.

In the idea deck are patience testers. Everything from monsters, contraptions, to your favorite TV show!

When coming up with an idea, you never know what you'll get. Idea cards are marked with a + or - on them and can either be played against a player, or on yourself.

When playing a - against a player, this will make their patience go down. Say you come up with a -3 patience card and you play it against someone. Their patience die will go down from 20 to 17.

Also in the deck, are + patience cards. If your patience die is at 17 and you play a +2, you will turn your die back to 19. Players may not go above 20. If a player reaches or goes past 0, the player has lost all patience and is mad. (They are out)

Promo cards (one for the players, one for the ghost) introduced to celebrate the release of Mysterium: Hidden Signs.

It features a wooden, untreated meeple in the weapon category (choke hazard).

The cards are designated P4.

Other Gods is the dueling card game that pits mythological gods of every tradition together in a pitched battle for divine supremacy. You play as the deity of your choice in the waning days of polytheism, desperately trying to retain your follower base while attempting to convert or destroy those of your opponents'. Win the game by being the last God with a following.

NOTE: For some weird reason, the expansion cards are 0.5cm narrower and slightly darker than the base game cards.

The modestly named The Game, billed as "The Superior Game of chance & skill" and "more exciting than backgammon", is played on a circular grid with numbered spaces. Players roll the dice and move pieces of their own as dictated by the results, trying to capture the pieces of other players. A player with no remaining pieces is out of the game, but until that happens, eliminated pieces can be brought back into the game by rolling doubles.

User summary
アミダ魚雷 (Amida Gyorai) means "Ghost Leg Torpedo" in English.

Place your Battle Ship and Ghost Leg panels in a good combination to hit the Monster by your torpedo(s). The player who manages to ring the Fire bell and strike the Monster with their torpedo(es) receives the "gem" left by the Monster.

The player who gathers the highest number of gems wins.

Publisher's summary
自分の「戦艦パネル」と「アミダパネル」をうまく繋げて、「カイジュウ」に魚雷を当てましょう。
