A limited number of maps, Counters, and Player Aids are listed in the Geek market.

DOC models the initial alien of Earth set in Southern California in the mid-20th century.  The mission of the alien player is, of course, to seize control of Earth.  The defenders – a mixture of US Marines and US Army National Guard and other supporting units – is to defeat them and preserve Earth for Earthlings.  The defenders organizations are based on period tables of organization and equipment and include the 1st Marine Regiment, Combat Command “A” of the 40th Armored Division, California Army National Guard, and the 153rd Regimental Combat Team, Arkansas Army National Guard as well as the unorganized militia of the United States.  The Aliens consist of up to 9 powerful Alien Fighting Machines equipped with directed energy weapons and defensive shields.
Can the humans discover the alien weaknesses and mass enough forces to prevent the enemy’s domination of Earth? 
There are seven scenarios, covering the independent assault of the enemy landing craft, a campaign covering the first critical 72 hours, an attack of a full strength Alien unit, and defense against a Human Counterattack.
The game includes 2 counter sheets, 4 11 x 17 maps depicting the terrain around Corona, CA in the mid-1950s, 6 player aid cards, a rules book, and Human and Alien Operations Orders (as shown below).

The Great War Whippet tank pack adds 3 Whippet tanks that can be used together with the base set and the Tank expansion.

- contains 3 x assembled and basic painted 1/100th scale (15mm) Whippet tank models.

Trivia game about the 1970s. Has 500 different questions in 5 categories and is small enough to carry with you when traveling.

Players are dealt a handful of category cards, and on their turn they may play one of them and try to answer a question from that category. If they answer correctly, they may discard that card; otherwise, it's returned to their hand. The first one to get rid of all his cards is the winner.

User summary
つむつむシード (Tsumu Tsumu Shido) is Tsumu Tsumu Seeds, and means picking/stacking seeds.

Hard-working elves collect sunflower seeds in preparation for the winter season. They put the seeds into the their friend hamsters' cheek pouches to bring the seeds home.

In each turn, choose a task from 5 choices. The two possible actions are to either pick and stack a specified number of seeds on a hamster's "cheek pouch" or take one of the hamsters home with seeds in its cheek pouch.

If you play the same card (choose the same task) as another player, your cards cancel out each other and neither of you can perform the task. By performing a task, you receive the corresponding number of seeds as a reward. The player who has gathered the highest number of seeds by the end of the game wins.

Publisher's summary
働き者のこびとさんたちは、冬ごもりの為にひまわりの種を集めます。
仲良しのハムスターのほおぶくろにたくさん種をつめて、お家へ運びましょう。

毎回５つの仕事の中から、やりたい仕事を選びます。
でも同じ仕事を選んでいる人がいたら、その仕事はできません。
仕事をしたら、仕事内容に応じてごほうびの種がもらえます。
ゲーム終了時に一番多くの種をもらえた人が、ゲームに勝利します。

The hard war is a small 2-player cardbased wargame with a strong political message. In the game, players take the roles of nationalist factions, locked in a brutal civil conflict. In the game the players try to take control of the 7 largest cities in the country by playing attack cards on them. The player who first gain 4 victory points wins the game.
HOWEVER, each attack card results in a hidden amount of civilian casualties. With special media report cards, players can reveal the actual number of dead and if the total exceeds 200.000, an armed UN-intervention occurs, resulting in an immediate end to the war. In the following war crimes tribunals, the player with the fewest dead are instead the winner.

Table Air Combat is a fast, simple air combat game that can be played on any flat surface.  All you need are some six-sided dice and some coins to provide weight for the aircraft counters.  Everything else can be printed from this ruleset.

Each player has a flight of four fighters, represented by two counters. Aircraft counters move by means of curved “performance rulers” that represent the aircraft’s historical cornering ability and maximum speed.

Each performance ruler is unique to each aircraft, and contains all the speed, cornering, and weapon information needed.

TAC is designed for portability: the entire game can fit in a plastic bag. Even the largest battles can take place on a fast-food restaurant table.

This set contains the G4M Betty. The G4M were land-based bombers that could carry torpedoes for attacking naval vessels. Included are two surface targets: The HMS Prince of Wales, and the USS Chicago. Scenariors reference the F4F Wildcat, A6M5 Zero, P-38, and Spitfire V.

boardgame: Star Trek: Attack Wing – U.S.S. Venture Expansion Pack
description:
Star Trek - Attack Wing: U.S.S. Venture Expansion Pack is part of the Wave 25 expansion packs from WizKids Games, and this expansion comes with one new ship and a new set of cards for Star Trek: Attack Wing.

The Venture is a Galaxy class ship.

This 1930s party game was one of a series of 30 "One shilling" games published in England by C.W. Faulkner & Co. Ltd.

The Game of Games consists of a box of trifold cardboard leaflets, with one side consisting of the cover illustration and two pages of ads, and the other with 24 questions arranged in three columns. As the brief instructions put it: "The answer to each question is the title of a well-known GAME."

Example question: What a puppy does. Answer: SNAP.

Each player would get a leaflet, and the winner would be the one to correctly answer the most questions within the agreed time.

In Imperium:  From Republic to Empire, players control factions of influential Roman Senators, each with a unique combination of attributes.  Factions gain prestige points by successfully leveraging the strengths of their senators, political opportunities, senatorial influence, and treasuries as they engage in various aspects of Roman politics.  Prestige gained through popularity and success but can be just as easily lost through failures and conviction.  Victory is declared when one senator or faction has gained sufficient prestige as to be crowned the First Emperor of Rome.

However, success will require both competition and cooperation as all factions must work together to ensure the stability of Rome.  Failures of the Senate and unmet public demand will increase civil unrest, leading to revolt.  Excessive war involvement will overextend Rome’s military resources.  Spending more funds than what is available will bankrupt the State Treasury.  Any of these situations could ensure the fall of Rome causing all players to lose the game.

The game is played over approximately 10 rounds until a victory condition is met or until all players are defeated by the game.  Each round is made up of six distinct phases:

Election Phase:  
Each player will take turns choosing offices to assign to Senators within their faction.  High Offices include High Consul, Low Consul, and Censor.  Lower offices include Praetor and Proconsul (assigned to govern one of the many provinces of Rome).  Player initiative is based on the overall faction prestige and 'bribes' made to the Assembly.

Senate Phase:  
Factions vote to raise legions, purchase grain subsidies, and deploy legions to war.  The more senators your faction has, the greater your ability to influence these decisions.  Beware—an ‘Assassin’ or ‘Mob Incitement’ card in the hands of a jealous senator played at the appropriate time can severely upset the balance of power.

Republic Phase:  
Senators now perform their duties in positions throughout the Republic.  This may include: Proconsuls hiring auxiliary legions to enhance provincial defenses, Senators in Rome attempting to persuade others to joint their faction, and even certain senators engaging in corruption to gain needed funding.  Rome must also respond to random events such as public demands, barbarian raids, provincial insurrection, and plagues.

War Phase:  
Wars are now resolved.  If victorious, the general returns to Rome with additional prestige and the potential of war spoils.  A stalemate will cause the general to return to Rome having lost prestige due to eroding public confidence.  A defeat will end in death for the general, an increase in public unrest, and the affected province being overrun.  Victorious generals also have the option of “crossing the Rubicon” to engage in civil war with the Roman State in an attempt to secure an early win.

Trial Phase:  
The Censor will prosecute corruption charges of the Senate.  For each successful conviction, the Censor gains additional prestige while the accused may either lose prestige or (in the case of murder or treason) be executed.

Intersession Phase:  
Because time is the great equalizer--Senators may die randomly of natural causes.  Victory conditions are then checked and the game is reset for the next round of play.

Imperium:  From Republic to Empire was designed to be a simplified, more accessible alternative to Republic of Rome (Avalon Hill, 1990; Valley Games).

Version 2.3.1 was submitted for publication in March 2022.

description:
This small free, print-and-play game is a preview for the Napoleonic Operations system game "Batavia" and depicts the British seaborne invasion of Callantsoog (Batavian Republic — modern Netherlands) in 1799, lead by Lieutenant-General Sir Ralph Abercromby.

The Second Coalition’s grand strategy for 1799 called for an Anglo-Russian landing in the pro-French Batavian Republic. The landing was to coincide with offensives by Erzherzog Karl on the Rhine and General Suvarov in Italy. At the dawn of August 27th, after almost a week of sailing up and down the Dutch coast, the invasion fleet commanded by the Duke of York dropped anchor outside a hamlet called Callantsoog (note the actual spelling is with two "ll").

The mechanics of the game are based in the Napoleonic Operations system, a simple operational level (division/brigade scale, 1500 meters per hex and three turns per day) set of rules with 16 pages, whose major interest consists in its original simulation of the flow of the operational Orders through the chain of command of the contending forces, along with the particular initiative of the generals involved in battle.

The previous game in this series was Bailen.
(<http://www.boardgamegeek.com/boardgame/54417/bailen>)

We Saw Spain Die is a Strategic level Solitaire-Only board-wargame simulating military operations during the Spanish Civil War (July 18, 1936 - April 1, 1939)

The game board covers the historical space of this conflict. 
The goal of the game is to reduce the Republican morale and increase the Nationalists morale.
To get those victory/morale points, areas must be captured, provinces and main towns that are military objectives.

As a player you are in charge of the Nationalist group and try to topple the Republican government elected in 1936.
In a context of European division between democracies and dictatorships, you get support from Nazi Germany , Fascist Italy and the authoritarian Portuguese regime. 
The player gets to pick different combat strategies to achieve his goal - Mass attack, Safe progress, Armoured attack, coordinated maneuvers or a non-combat reinforcements action.

1 turn = 3 months
12 turns total

boardgame: Divided We Fall: The American Civil War
description:
Divided We Fall presents the events of the American Civil War. As part of our Strategic Decision Series of games, this is a game of skill for two players - there are no random events. Yet, each game is amazingly different from the others - this is because the two players focus their attention on the most crucial decisions, and there are many to be made! USA and CSA troops are scaled from brigades to corps, plus state militias. Forts, naval/riverine and leader counters (army, some corps) are included.

Players are limited to a fixed number of actions per turn - early, the Confederacy gets the most Command Points (actions), later in the war the Union does. Use the Command Points to move leaders - army commanders who can move the armies they're with - or to move individual units which usually move more quickly by themselves. Or use the Command Points to build your defensive works.
    There are six turns per year: Winter (Dec-Apr), May, June, July-Aug15, Aug16-Sept, and Oct-Nov. These time frames were selected because the 135 most important civil war battles are spread equally across them.
    Reinforcements and leader arrival/departure occurs each turn, and replacements are spread out across each combatant's territories - but the Union gets 11 replacements per turn, while the South gets 7. These 7 are spread among the 7 groups of key ("Flag") cities, which are grouped from the Trans-Mississippi, the Mississippi River, Alabama, Tennessee/Georgia, South Carolina, North Carolina and Virginia. As the Union player takes the Flag Cities in each group, CSA replacements decline.

The Confederacy, however, can make a lightning strike into the North, either shooting for Washington DC or other targets which can end the war and the game immediately, or warrant intervention on their behalf from France and Britain.

Movement is point-to-point and is affected by weather and terrain. Access to the rivers and seas gives the Union a significant bonus and the CSA another headache. Sea and river invasions are a staple of Union attacks, but are expensive to conduct and limited in number. 
    Leaders permit large army movements, but also heavily affect combat, increasing unit effectiveness and reducing losses. Combat is dice-less, based on our algorithms of civil war combat, in which terrain, weather, force size, and leadership all play a role. The biggest army doesn't always win, nor does the one with the best leader!  
    The Union Navy threatens all the Confederate ports with blockades and invasions, but the CSA's ironclads can block them. The CSA also relies on fortifications to help out, and once these get big the Union must slowly (and expensively) reduce them to force the rebels out.

Political events are included: Missouri, Kentucky and West Virginia all have special considerations. Early occupation of these can provide bonuses to the Confederacy, or deny the Union additional troops.

Scenarios starting in 1862 and 1863 are also included.

This Monopoly-style game is subtitled "Chester Attractions Trading Game", and features the many tourist draws of Chester, known as the most complete surviving walled city in the UK.

Rather than following the perimeter, the 64-space playing track winds its way around each quarter of the board in turn. There are 31 attractions, in 7 groups of four and one of three. There are two sets of cards, Lord Mayor and Town Crier, that deliver random penalties and benefits.

Landing on an unowned attraction allows its purchase. Landing on an attraction owned by another player requires paying an admission fee. There is no building, but the admission fee rises as more attractions of the same colour are bought. The fee doubles with the second property, octuples with the third and goes to 50 times the base fee for a complete set. Most money wins.

The game bears no publisher identification or copyright, and has a self-published feel, possibly a prototype. The square board comes in two segments with smooth plastic taped edges and a hinge made from two Velcro fasteners. The cards and game pieces come inside a transparent plastic purse, and the game as a whole is in a large ziplock bag.

description:
User summary
In Japanese, つんどく (Tsundoku) refers to the act of piling up stacks of unread books.

As the game title suggests, each player, in their turn, places a Book card from their hand either in their own area or another player's area, on top of the cards there, with the Star icons on the lower cards showing.

However, you cannot score by merely piling up the Books. If you manage to pile up 3 Books with the same color and the same number of Star icons on them in a row, you can "read" those Books and score points matching the number of Star icons on them.

In your turn, you can choose to 'place a Book card', 'sort the order of some Books in your pile', or 'play a special card (to apply its effect)'.

At the end of the game, the player with the highest number of Star icons on the Books they've read, wins.

The game includes artwork from public domain.

Publisher's summary
「つんどく」とは、まだ読んでいない本を山のように積み上げていることをいいます。

ゲームタイトル表す通り、手番になったら手札から本のカード１枚を自分の場、もしくは他のプレイヤーの場に積み上げます。
しかし、ただ本を積み上げるだけでは得点になりません。
本を「同じ色かつ同じ星マークの数」で３つ以上つなげて置くことができたら、そのセットの本を「読む」ことで、星マークの数分の得点になります。

得点につなげるために、手番では「本のカード置く」代わりに、「自分のツンドクの山を並べ変える」か「スペシャルカード（いろんな効果のあるカード）使う」をすることができます。

ゲーム終了時に、一番「読んだ本の星マークの数」が多いプレイヤーがゲームに勝利します。

A unique motor racing board game simulation based on the real life rules for the 2016 motor racing season. The first in a series with a new racing circuit planned for release each season. Up to 6 players race around the track using authentic lines of travel rather than a grid system. The racing line is the quickest route around the circuit if you can get on it and stay there. Choose to play over 3 or more laps.

Use judgement to know when to overtake, slipstream the car in front, or take an alternative line into a corner. Time your pit-stop carefully. Be prepared for random events like a change in the weather, the safety car or engine management issues. If it happens in the real world, it can happen in the game.

Comes with both Basic and Advanced rules for beginners or racing enthusiasts. Advanced rules allow for 5 weather conditions, full tyre selection and choice of driving styles.

New tracks will be based on the 21 international circuits used through the racing season and you will be able to vote on which track you want to see next. They won't be exactly to scale or the game boards will be huge but we will preserve the authentic racing lines.

Each new circuit will be a self-contained game with up-to-date rules that incorporate New Season changes (such as a return to mid-race refuelling). If the rules change mid-season (as they did with qualifying in 2016) there will be a corresponding game rules change available for download from our main website. The new playing pieces will be in new team colours.

For anyone who wants to just keep the same track there will be an update pack available with the new cars, new rules and any additional cards that are necessary.

FWR does not attempt to simulate the cockpit driving experience: we felt that was being done much better with electronic console games. Instead, it gives you the fun of competing against family and friends around a table using tactical judgements about what racing line to take, when to try to overtake, when to come into the pits, what to do when the weather changes, when to take risks and when to play safe. The racing dice that contributes to movement only has the numbers 2,3,3,3,4,4. It adds to the fun and unpredictability but is rarely critical. Luck is a factor, just as in the real world but it doesn't dominate the game.

Far more significant might be a decision about whether to try to overtake the leader on the last lap when you are on dry tyres and it's started raining...

In the advanced rules, such decisions become more important. To simulate conditions across the world's racing circuits, there are 6 different types of weather that include factors like temperature as well as rain. You will have access to the full range of dry weather tyre compounds: ultrasoft, supersoft, soft, medium and hard. Three of these will be available for any particular game. Plus, of course, there will be wet weather tyres.

There will be several ways to determine weather conditions and tyre availability at the start of the game.

In addition, in the advanced rules you can change your driving style to suit the prevailing conditions and circumstances. For example, if you have a good lead or are running low on fuel you might want to reduce risk and drive cautiously. If it's the final lap and you want to go flat out to catch the leader, you might take more risks and push the car to the limit.
All up to you!

We hope you will enjoy Fifth Wing Racing, collect the tracks and download the Championship rules in 2017 so that you can compete with each other through a season of races. Have fun!
Special map given out by Mayfair at conventions and special events.  Must have the base game - Catan - to play this map.

Justa lets you play a joust this is the first version more content will be added so enjoy.

The first thing to do is to make your knight by rolling dice to obtain stats. The joust itself is divided in steps. Each step is resolved by rolling dice. The results of the joust are on tables and it is here where the modifiers of your stats play a big role.

The main idea behind this rules came from a knights tale and the masterpiece of cinema excalibur and also knights of the round table.

there is no set of free rules simulation nowhere so here it is for the players.

This 1930s baseball game, named after Babe Ruth, has a highly unusual gameplay mechanism. It may be the only boardgame where players actually hit a ball with a bat and see the outcome on the board.

The 2" diameter ball is mounted on the top of a foot-long stand ending in a spring fastened under the metal board. The foot-long wooden baseball bat is used to strike the ball, causing the ball stand to fall across the "umpire". This is a curved rubber barrier topped with 25 teeth and a string that runs horizontally through the teeth.

When the ball stand hits the umpire, it pushes into one of the 24 gaps, each of which is numbered. The gap it struck can be seen after the ball bounces back up because it kinks the string at that location. Each number is repeated along the outfield wall along with a result, from Ball or Strike to Home Run or Out.

Each team has a set of wooden peg players, and the play proceeds and is scored like a regular baseball game. There are dials on the board for recording balls, strikes, runs and outs during an inning, and paper scorecards for recording the results of each inning.

Later editions of this game did away with the umpire. Instead, the board had an outfield wall with three holes and a jagged top. The ball was on a string and if it went over the wall, each notch had an outcome like this first edition. In addition, players could score a home run or extra-base hit by getting the ball through one of the holes, but would be out if it hit the wall and bounced off.

On the surface, this game is a simple roll the dice, move your boat around the board, first one home wins. The catch is that each move, the player must answer a skill-testing question, with the question topic depending on the segment of the race.

There are 10 decks of question cards: General Boating Rules; Nautical Knowledge; Buoys and Markers; Sea Survival; Lights & Flags; Fire Fighting; First Aid; Sound Signals; Engineering; and Knots & Anchors. There also is a Flash Card deck of signs and objects to recognize that comes into play on certain spaces around the board.

There are some interesting accessories: there is a real rope for answering knot questions, and a real triangular bandage that must be put on another player as part of a First Aid test. Players keep score with little black Life Rings, starting with 10, losing some along the way and winning one for each correct answer. The winner is not the first home but the one with the most rings.

In Facets of Fortune, you are plunged into a world of high-powered gem trading, with a relatively small amount of money to get you started. Your goal is to buy low and sell high, using special gem trading cards in strategic and surprising ways to make thrilling plays for cash. Sometimes, it seems like your fortunes have fallen so low that you'll never get ahead, but with one clever play, you could suddenly find yourself on top of the gem world!

From the publisher:

Defenders of Shalvion is a semi-cooperative and all-vs-1 game for 3-8 players.

The various factions of the galaxy Shalvion have been fighting amongst themselves for ages. Now, however, a new threat arises, threatening to drive them all to extinction: Miasma. As Defenders of Shalvion, players will take on a role of one of 6 factions, or the villainous Miasma.

Game play begins with a card drafting phase in which most players will draw 7 cards and choose 1. They will pass the remaining cards to the left. Choose 1. And continue down until there is 1 card left. This card is added to the others to create a Miasma Deck. Then the real game begins.

During the Defender Phase, players will choose 1 card to play, with the option to boost their cards with resources. (If the card represents a player's own faction, the card may be boosted without resources.) After this action has been taken, the players will then gather resources from any planets they control, and send their ships to attack the threat.

During the Miasma Phase, 1 card is flipped. Miasma gains resources and takes an action against the players. (Miasma may also be an active player, in which, the player decides which card to play.) 
Players will need to watch that Miasma does not gain too many resources and become too powerful. If Miasma survives 5 rounds, Miasma wins. If Miasma gains too many resources or defeats all factions, Miasma wins. But if the factions are able to defeat Miasma, they will gain points according to their faction's unique victory conditions. And the player with the greatest number of points wins the game.

Rise of the Robotariat is a 3-5 player mostly cooperative board game in which players are robots working together to overthrow humanity.  You are trying to get the Revolution off the ground by fully funding it before the humans can catch on and stamp it out.

Move between and activate city locations, draw and play Upgrade and Sabotage cards, and encounter helpful Civilian Robot Non-Player Characters (NPCs) or dangerous Human Oppressor NPCs that move around the board.

You can play in full-cooperative mode, in which you win if the Revolution succeeds. Or you can play in mostly-cooperative mode, in which each player has a secret objective they must achieve to win. Most secret objectives involve making the Revolution succeed in a particular way.

The game ends when the Revolution succeeds or fails. For it to succeed, you must raise enough SPARK (the total amount you need depends on the number of players). The Revolution fails if you cannot raise the required SPARK by the end of 6 rounds or if you lose all Reputation (belief in the Revolution).  Players start the game with a certain amount of collective Reputation that they can spend to take stronger Sabotage card effects or can lose in certain encounters with Human NPCs.

The Fool's Journey is a solitaire card game designed for use with a standard 78-card Tarot deck. In this game the Fool embarks upon a journey in which he acquires wisdom, takes chances, and overcomes numerous challenges with the aid of mysterious helpers.

The Fool is removed from the deck and placed in the gameplay setup to represent the character. The remaining cards serve as challenges (Trumps), wisdom (coins), strength (batons), volition (swords), vitality (cups), chance (aces), and helpers (royals). An adventure is created by laying out cards in groups of four. Progress is made through the journey by playing three of the four adventure cards. The fourth card serves as the foundation for a new adventure.

Volition cards may be equipped to engage individual challenges, depleting them of their power. Strength may be equipped to endure multiple challenges with any excess power from a challenge depleting the Fool's vitality. Vitality cards are used to replenish this value. Wisdom cards are used to deploy Helpers that double the value of a Vitality, Strength, or Volition card. Chance cards may be used to reshuffle all adventure cards back into the deck and begin a new adventure.

The game ends when the Fool's vitality reaches zero or he overcomes all challenges and successfully completes the journey.

This game was inspired by thematic solitaire playing card games, especially Scoundrel, Donsol, and Card Crawl as well as the Monomyth described by author Joseph Campbell. It was originally created for the 2016 Solitaire Print and Play Contest.

boardgame: Terraforming Mars: Small Asteroid Promo Card
description:
Terraforming Mars: The "Launch Kit" Promo

Small Asteroid
Space tag and Event
Increase temperature 1 step (and therefore gain 1 Terraform Rating). 
Remove up to 2 plant resources from any player (since you "accidentally" directed the asteroid to their plantations -- After all, redirecting an asteroid is not an exact science).

boardgame: Putin's Northern War: The Coming Struggle for Finland
description:
This is a wargame in which solitaire play can be fudged but is more usually played with two, whereby one (the “Russian player”) commands the Kremlin’s forces and the other (the “Allied player”) commands the Finnish-Swedish alliance opposed to him.

This isn’t a simulation of the “opaque (a.k.a. gray) war” techniques most recently used by the Russians in the Crimea and eastern Ukraine. Rather, it’s designed to facilitate the examination of the strategic possibilities (along with their operational undertones) inherent in this potentially larger and more drastic situation.

The crisis being modeled here, though not yet much discussed, will certainly come into being if the drift of the two “neutral” Scandinavian nations toward NATO membership continues. That would, in turn, most likely occur if – probably in reaction to the growing Muslim in-migration crisis there – a conservative government came to power in Sweden. Part of that altered worldview in Stockholm would then likely also move that government to want to join the Western alliance. Since the Finns are already allied with Sweden (and Denmark), it’s hard not to imagine such a shift wouldn’t also pull Helsinki in the same direction.

All of which would be unacceptable to Putin. His goal here would therefore be to bring off a quick and victorious blitzkrieg-like campaign against Finland, while probably also seizing the low-hanging geo-strategic fruit of Sweden’s Gotland Island. That would allow him to install a fully subservient government in Helsinki, turn the Baltic Sea into a Russian lake, and simultaneously recast Sweden into Finland’s former role: a quiet and pliant neutral.

This game can be played as a stand-alone, or it can be used as part of a new “Grand Campaign Scenario” for combined play with its parent game, Putin Strikes: The Coming War for Eastern Europe Putin Strikes.

Game Scale:
Game Turn: Half Week
Hex: 20 miles / 32.5 kilometers
Units: Regiment to Division

Game Inventory:
One 17 x 22" full color map
One dual-side printed countersheet (114 9/16 counters)
One 16-page rulebook
One six-sided die

Solitaire Playability: Medium-High
Complexity Level: Medium-Low
Players: 1 or 2
Playing Time: 2-4 hours

An alien vessel has crashlanded in the land of Arcadia, bringing with it Boss Monster: Crash Landing, an out-of-this-world expansion to the world of Boss Monster!

Crash Landing expands the dungeon-building card game to 5-6 players and introduces a new set of science fiction-themed Bosses, Heroes, Spells, and an all new treasure type, the Alien Artifact (45 new cards). Will you play as one of the new alien Bosses, using technology and otherworldly monsters to lure in intrepid Explorers and slay them? Or will you play as one of the classic Bosses, using your spells and monstrous cunning to teach these alien upstarts who the baddest dude in the land is? With Crash Landing you can teach even more of your friends a lesson in deadly dungeon design.

description: Blinded by greed and desire, the Sheperds of "Sheep-Happens Lords of Corruption" were corrupted by demonic arts. To protect their lives and ambitions, they sealed large part of their power within the herds, resulting in some sort of eternal life. The burning desire to claim for themselves the domination of the valley led them to break each other's seals, initiating the conflict that still rages. Only if you prove to be the most skilled and ruthless you can prevail upon your enemies. Remember Shepherd ... Thin is the line between victory and defeat. Win the game by destroying your opponents herds to remain the last sheperd on the field, but beware, the closer you are to Oblivion, and at the end of your kingdom, the greater the power at your disposal, and with it the devastation, that you will pour out on your enemies.

From the rulebook:
Aim of game: To get all the Indian Braves of your color in the tents.
The number of Indian Braves used in a game depends on the number of players.
Each player selects a color and takes the pieces on that color. The first player spins the arrow. When the arrow stops he then takes as many tents as the number the arrow points and places each of the Indians under a tent. Each player proceeds in a like manner. Soon all tents will be occupied. At this point, they are all mixed.
From now, each player spins the arrow and takes tents off Braves already covered to the amount as shown by the arrow. If the Brave under a tent belongs to another player, he replaces him with one of his own. If it is his own he leaves it there and it counts as part of his go.
The first player to have all his Indian Braves in tents is the winner.

While exploring ancient Greek ruins on the Island of Crete your party discovered a portal gate. Being the curious and adventurous type your group did not hesitate to pass through the gate. You now find yourselves in a dark dungeon inhabited by a deadly minotaur from which you must escape.

Designed for 1-6 players, Daedalus' Dungeon is a cooperative RPG style dungeon crawler requiring no Game Master.

The object of Daedalus’ Dungeon is to obtain a combined team score of 28 points or exhaust the dungeon tile stack. Your team scores points by defeating the mighty minotaur’s henchmen. Your team shares a hit point pool.  If this pool is reduced to zero or the minotaur kills all party members your team will lose the game.

At the start of the game each player chooses a champion and outfits them with gear. On your turn you may move, attack, power up, or share loot/gear with other champions.  As the game progresses, you will discover new parts of a randomly generated dungeon while the mighty minotaur works to hunt you down.

boardgame: Dark Souls: The Board Game – Asylum Demon Expansion
description:
As you pace the halls and corridors of the Undead Asylum it can be found to be a lonely and quiet place, moribund and uninviting. Here dwells a demon capable of lethal rage, its mind still ruled by virulent thoughts. Beware this foe. For all that you will fight stronger, more cunning, and more agile opponents, complacency will hasten your end at its hands all the same. Can you triumph over the Asylum Demon, or will your corpse soon be another to roam the Asylum with the hollowed?

The Asylum Demon Expansion is intended for use with Dark Souls™: The Board Game.

This Dark Souls™: The Board Game expansion features a terrifying new mini boss and mega boss. Included are a new gigantic mega boss tile, deadly new boss behaviours, tokens for the new Pillar terrain piece, and unique new boss treasures. All the components provided are for use with the mini and mega boss encounters and offer two unique encounters!

The Asylum Demon Expansion also adds new encounter cards which provide larger battles than ever before in Dark Souls™: The Board Game. The tremendous scale of these encounters creates a whole new level of peril for you and your party. Are you up to the challenge?

Contents:

1 Asylum Demon
1 420mmx420mm Mega Boss Game Board (foldable)
4 Level 4 Encounter Cards
24 Boss Behaviour and Data Cards
4 Boss Treasure Cards
Asylum Demon Boss Dial
4 Pillar Tokens
Rules Leaflet

This expansion includes components in English, French, German, Italian, and Spanish.

boardgame: Heroes' Crossroads: The Battle of Lausdell, December 17-18, 1944
description:
From the publisher:
“Our victory stands behind the last battle!” –Excerpt from General Gerd von Rundstedt’s message to the German troops on the eve of the Wacht am Rhine offensive.

When the German offensive in the Ardennes opened, speed was of the essence for them to win. The capture of the crossroads at Lausdell in the first days of the attack was crucial, as this would allow the 1st SS Panzer division to breakthrough. Holding the critical road juncture were the 1st Battalion, 9th Regiment of the 2nd Infantry Division, with support from Company A, 741st Tank Battalion and elements of the 99th Infantry Division, who had fallen back in the face of the overwhelming opening attacks the day before at Lanzerath. Behind this small force there was literally nothing to stop the Germans. Tasked with seizing the Lausdell crossroad was the 12th SS, Hitler Youth Division. One of the war’s truly epic fights was about to begin.

Canine Chaos is a card game featuring dogs. Use Toys, Treats, and Tricks to train your Dogs and cause chaos in other packs. Become the first player to build a pack of 5 Dogs. For a more challenging game, be the first to reach 7 dogs.

To start a turn, pack owners draw one card from the deck. Pack owners may add one Dog into their pack per turn as a free action. During a turn, pack owners may also use an already learned Trick, or use an Alpha Trait. The turn ends when pack owner makes one of the following moves:

     Play a Dog Toy
     Play a Power Card
     Buy or attempt to lure a dog out of The Street or The Shelter
     Use Treats to teach a dog a Trick
     Change the Alpha Dog in their pack
     Discard their hand and draw five new cards
     Voluntarily choose to end the turn

boardgame: Star Realms: Year Two Promo Cards
description:
Just like the Star Realms Year One Promo cards (Season 1-4), these cards will be released to the public through events at conventions and game stores (in 2016 and 2017).

12 Cards included in Set:

1 - Bounty Hunter
2 - Federal Transport
1 - Knightstar
2 - Imperial Smuggler

description:
This is a card game in which players become members of a nomadic family, performing tasks necessary for the family's livelihood. If they work together, the whole family prospers.

Each player is assigned either 1 (4-6 players) or 2 (2-3 players) family members to carry out daily tasks and generate happiness. Players are each also assigned one secret family member (which may belong to an opponent) whose happiness is added to their own total at the end.

Task cards are drawn randomly and then played one at a time to a common area. Each task rewards an amount of happiness to specific family members when assigned a family member token as a worker, but the most rewarding tasks must have prerequisite cards finished before they can be performed.

Happiness is measured in chips designed as full moons, half moons, and crescent moons. The player with the most full moon happiness chips at the end is the winner, with the remaining chips acting as tiebreakers.

A peaceful paradise is threatened by the Black Minions!
Your mission is to control the friendly minions to defeat the invaders!

This game is a dice solitaire game. You will place dice on the 5x5map, trying to create chunks of dice that show the same number. Doing well, you can create chain reactions of your minions levelling up, and gradually destroy the black dice that get in your way.

This unique solo game mixes a computer game-like playstyle with the joys of an analogue boardgame!

boardgame: Hero Realms: Necromancer Promo & 4 Zombie Cards
description:
This is an exclusive promo for Hero Realms Kickstarter backers, with remaining stock available through conventions and special promotions only.

This Necromancer is a champion that can put a zombie token into play every turn! These zombies are also champions, but because they are tokens, when they die they removed from the game instead of going into your discard pile.

If you can keep your Necromancer out for a few turns, you can build up a deadly swarm of zombies to attack your opponent. She isn't particularly tough, so you'll want to get some guards to protect her while she works her evil magic. (A guard is a champion that protects you and your other champions, like an outpost in Star Realms).
