The person who distributes draws 5 cards from the deck and reveals them. Divide these cards into two sets, such as 2 and 3, or 1 and 4, and add magic carpet cards to make a total of 4 cards.

The other player chooses one of the two decks of cards and proceeds to lay out the cards while revealing each other's hands.

If you make one card too strong, your opponent will naturally take that combination, so it is necessary to distribute it in a well-balanced manner. How to sort is very important.

Your hand information is always open to the public, so you'll have to think about which card to place in which area, and you'll be faced with a variety of choices when it comes to not only distributing cards but also placing them. This is the biggest attraction of this game.

The rules themselves are difficult enough for anyone to understand after playing it once, so if you have many opportunities to play board games with two people, this is a board game we recommend you play once!

A yellow-themed, replayablity-increasing expansion to A.D.A.P.T. Includes 15 new genetically-boosted guppies and 3 bonus guppies (red, blue, and green). Also introduces changeling, ferocious, genetic engineer, hearty, lethal, metamorphosis, oversize: (location), survivalist, and time turning.

A black-themed biomechanical expansion to A.D.A.P.T. Includes 8 new parts, 3 new guppies, and 4 resistance trackers. Also introduces genetic boosts, blast, destined, energized, oversize: (location), shapeshift, shielded, targeting, and transmogrify.

A hand sensing, light changing, exciting modular game system complete with 5 different modes! Wave your hand over the top to activate the 5 different sections. The 5 modes are Catch Me, Join, Remember, Follow, and DJ

Catch Me: A target color will appear. The five sections will blink on and off with different color lights. Every time the target color appears, move your hand over it to score!

Join: Your goal is to connect the two like colors that appear in the set of five within a few seconds. Wave your hand from the first color to the second to paint The Wave with that color and score!

Remember: A sequence of five lights will appear. After a few seconds the sequence will shuffle itself. By waving your hand from section to section, you can exchange the positions of colors. Recreate the original sequence in time to score!

Follow: The Wave will say a target color and present you with five lights. Wave your hand over the correct one to move to the next level. Each level's difficulty increases by increasing the amount of colors you need to remember in order while decreasing the time and shuffling up the lights each time you hit a correct color!

Make Music: A free play mode where you can make your own jams! Wave your hand over a section to turn it on, triggering a music loop to play until the section is turned off. Hold your hand over a section to play an alternate loop of the same type. Combine different music loops and explore the beginnings of becoming a new DJ!

Contains all 5 of the 17-card expansions for A.D.A.P.T. in one box. Includes the Bio-Mech, Cephalopod, Fantasy, Reptile & Amphibian, and Guppy expansion packs. Also introduces Genetic Boosts, Blast, Destined, Energized, Oversize: (location), Shapeshift, Shielded, Targeting, Transmogrify, Autotomy, Grab, Inking, Neg Lethality, Changeling, Immune: Poison, Shapeshifting, Ferocious, Genetic Engineer, Hearty, Lethal, Metamorphosis, Survivalist, and Time Turning, and Withdraw. Adds +3 players to the base game.

"You have stumbled upon the news that there is a fire sale going on at a bankrupt estate a few blocks away. They are selling priceless antiques off at insane prices! You gathered all the cash you could and ran to the now defunct mansion. Little did you know however that tons of people would show up, all with huge wads of cash, ready to outbid you on all these “priceless” treasures!"

Fire Sale is playable at home with 2 decks of cards, 5 dice per player, and rules (in Files section). Fire Sale is a 2-4 player game featuring auction/bidding based dice placement/manipulation and set collection. Over multiple rounds players must use their dice rolls wisely to try and buy cards from the center of the table to earn sets that they can trade in for victory points!

The first player is chosen by whoever rolls the lowest amount of the lowest die value and on each turn a player has 7 possible actions to choose from, 5 being their available "main" actions and 2 being possible free actions. These actions include the ability to buy cards (to form sets), bid on cards against your opponents, play cards from your hand, turn in cards to earn points, or discard cards to get new cards in your hand. The free actions include re-rolling and "flipping" your dice. The game requires efficient use of a player's five dice that are rolled each round, in which "opposite" faced die are very valuable, though each player has ways to influence their dice rolls in tactical ways to suit their needs.

During the game players make moves with their dice, play cards from their hand, and turn in their collected sets for points. Players can use the point "pool" wisely to gain extra points while also blocking their opponents from gaining extra points. The player with the most points at the end of the game wins!

boardgame: Hail Caesar:  The Conquest of Gaul
description:
Hail Caesar is a game of tabletop battles with model soldiers set in the Ancients era. Pit your wits and tactics against your friends as your armies clash in combat on the tabletop.

The Conquest of Gaul starter set gives you two starter forces the barbaric Celts and the legions of Imperial Rome and the glorious Hail ­Caesar rulebook.

This boxed game is just the start of your journey to conquer all comers and write your name in the annals of history!

Hail ­Caesar - Conquest of Gaul contains:

Full-colour 192 ­page hardback Hail ­Caesar rulebook

94 multi-pose, hard plastic 28mm miniatures:

30 Imperial ­Roman Legionaries
    Roman Scorpion catapult and crew
    60 Celt Warriors

boardgame: Pike & Shotte: For King and the Country
description:
Pike & Shotte is a game of tabletop battles with model soldiers set in the 17th century. Pit your wits and tactics against your friends as armies clash in massed combat.

This starter set gives you the splendid Pike & Shotte rulebook and two starter forces field them as Royalist or Parliamentarian in the English Civil Wars or as one of the many protagonists involved in the immense Thirty Years War.

This boxed game is only the beginning, as you battle all-comers and reinforce your army further in the pursuit of glory...

Pike & Shotte - For King & Country contains:

Full-colour 208-page hardback (Pike & Shotte Core Rulebook)

82 multi-pose, hard plastic 28mm miniatures:

58 pike and musket including command
    12 Cavalry
    12 infantry with Firelocks

The first promo card for the Adventure card game Aventuria. Its text reads:

Würfelst du bei einer Probe oder einem Angriff mit dem W20 eine 1, darfst du 2 statt 1 Karte vom Zugstapel ziehen. Würfelst du eine 20, musst du keine Handkarte ablegen.

The card's text roughly translates as:

"If you roll a 1 on a D20 while attacking or checking a success you may draw two cards instead of one. If you roll a 20, you do not have to discard a card."

Queen Strike combines a 3D holographic display with a traditional board game to bring you a new and exciting interactive experience. As little heroes of the "Music Kingdom", your quest is to collect musical notes in the kingdom and complete the fabled "Rainbow Score". Begin your adventure from one of the four starting points. Overcome treacherous traps set by the "Queen of Hearts" as she seeks discord! Roll the dice, explore different areas, and thwart the queen's plan by defeating her guards. Get to the castle, undo the queen's magic spell, and save the kingdom!

Queen Strike is a co-operative game. Players take turns rolling the movement dice and moving their hero pawn along the game map to collect the musical notes. They might encounter the Queen's guard and enter the "combat mode" displayed in the 3D holographic platform (driven by a game app on the player's own tablet), in which players need to use their combat cards to defeat the guard (in a rock-paper-scissors system). Players might also run into some event cards that bring them some surprise animation in the holographic movie. Once the players collect enough musical notes, they may challenge the Queen to win the game!

Description from the publisher:

Jamaica: The Crew is a set of twenty characters, each with a special power, that add flavor to the game without any big changes in the rules. The characters may be hired when you're able to pay for the fee of a harbor, and they are "loaded" like any other resource, following the same rules.

You might find it enticing to add several characters to your boat — especially since most of them earn you additional gold at the end of the game — but with fewer holds dedicated to the regular resources, navigation becomes more dicey. All in all, more challenge, more silly fun, more Jamaica.

boardgame: Ghostbusters: The Board Game – Impossible Mode Taxi Drivers
description: Two impossible mode red mini figures and card that was given to Kickstarter backers.

Orléans: Trade & Intrigue is the second large expansion for the award-winning game Orléans.

This expansion adds new place tiles and four new modules:

     Orders: a new set of cards, each depicting goods and a city - collect the goods and turn them in at the city for victory points
     New Events: a completely new set of 34 Hour Glass Tiles from which 18 are semi-randomly chosen for each game
     New Beneficial Deeds: a replacement Beneficial Deeds board providing completely new rewards for sending away your Followers
     Intrigue: a replacement Beneficial Deeds board allowing you to attack and hinder your opponents or even steal from them

Munchkin Playmat: Spyke's Christmas Wish is a 28"×14" playmat that includes spots to keep track of your Class, Race, level, and all your monster-slaying gear, and it features an in-game bonus: You can draw an item from the treasure discard pile for free once per game; you've been good this year!

The Munchkin Christmas Monster Box has room for two thousand Munchkin cards, various Kill-O-Meters, a Level Playing Field or two, Dungeons, Seals, and more. To get you started, it includes six Munchkin promotional bookmarks and twelve Munchkin promo cards.

boardgame: The Order of Vampire Hunters: Tori Zaibatsu Expansion
description:
This expansion for The Order of Vampire Hunters includes:


     4 Hunters: Isamu, Kukiko, Akihiko, and Rai
     4 ID cards
     4 weapon cards
     4 Bonus cards


This Hunter set tells the story of Osamu Himori, the founder of Tori Zaibatsu, and his children.

Isamu glanced at Akihiko. “If this is for the family, perhaps Kukiko should be here.” His voice trailed away.

“No, little brother. You saw father’s instructions. We were to be alone when you open the box. We’ve been lieutenants in this company for long enough. Himori-san groomed us well to take over in his stead. Now, open it and read the will.” Akihiko’s voice was firm but he touched his brother’s wrist reassuringly.

Isamu gasped as he looked into the box. An antique tanto, stained with blood lay upon a handwritten page. This was clearly not the legal volume they were expecting to help them divide their father’s multinational.

Isamu gingerly extracted the page and began to read, “Sons, you have honored me, but I have misled you. I have hidden the story of my ancestors and you think your martial arts training was to give you discipline and honor in the boardroom. If I am not here to tell you the story myself, you will find this hard to believe. Now that Night has come, Tori is worthless compared to your true legacy...

****

Rai put down her pen and wondered how Osamu Himori’s children would react to the change. Like the rest of the world, they thought of her as Himori-san’s secretary. Would the truth be too much for them? Not all of Himori-san’s business dealings were above board, especially in the early days. Now there was more espionage than bone breaking, but no one suspected the woman taking notes at Himori-san’s side also managed Tori’s seedy underbelly. Forever Osamu’s confidante, she knew one day she might have to lead the boys on a much darker path than she had ever travelled. The Night ... who could believe those stories? And how could she keep poor Kukiko in the dark? She must never know what took her father and was bound to take her brothers.

****

Kukiko tapped her earpiece and put it on her dresser. Too strong to be pushed aside because she was a woman, she had taken her future into her own hands long ago. Some may call it spying, she preferred to think of it as preparation for her role in the family. She did not need to hear the rest of the will, she had discovered her father’s secrets long ago.

Mechanically, she applied her white face paint, a simple disguise she took from her name. She picked up her tessen and began her kata.

A light party game built around intriguing prompts for lively conversation.

Each player is dealt three cards from the deck. Cards show 1, 2, or 3 points in the bottom lefthand corner, indicating the depth or gravity of the card. A 1-point card might ask, "What's the worst advice you ever received?" A 2-point card might ask, "Is it okay to cry at work?" A 3-point card might ask, "What happens after we die?"

All players select one card and play it facedown in the center of the table. The cards are shuffled and revealed, and everyone points to the card they want to discuss (with the caveat that players are not allowed to vote for their own card). Tied votes are resolved through discussion until a player changes their vote and creates a majority.

When your card is selected, you receive the number of points shown on the chosen card. The game ends when the deck is played through, and the player with the most points wins!

Description from the publisher:

You and your fellow midshipmen are fresh recruits on a British frigate heading to the West Indies. As part of your training, you have been tasked with the navigation of the ship. Compete against your peers to see who can guide the ship on the best course.

In As Seen in the Sky, each player must use their skill to reveal stars and gain way to the vessel's destination. As you practice, you will gain skill and learn tricks to outwit your fellow midshipmen. Earn points while revealing stars and if you can, reveal your hidden constellations to give you a step up on the competition. There will be many challenges in your wake before this trip is over, with squalls and seas that will push your limits. Many can learn the trade, but only one of you can expect a promotion. Who will it be?

On your turn, the player will roll a die to see what the current sea conditions are. Then based on the sea state, the player can use talents they have acquired to manipulate tiles in the night sky, move talents around on their player board, lock visible star coordinates in the sky, gain skill for adding talents to their arsenal, or gain course points for getting your ship closer to its destination. Before handing the die to the next player, you can take one visible unlocked tile from the night sky and place it in their play area, paying the skill needed for the placement. The game ends when all the tiles in the sky have been revealed. Players then reveal their completed constellations to gain bonus course points and the player with the highest course points is declared the winner.

boardgame: Star Wars: Armada – The Corellian Conflict
description:
Description from the publisher:

Join your friends in open warfare to decide the fate the Corellian Sector! The Corellian Conflict campaign expansion for Star Wars: Armada allows you and up to five of your friends to wage an epic campaign spanning multiple fleet battles. Take sides with the Galactic Empire or the Rebel Alliance, then vie for key hyperlane routes, seize vital shipyards, and establish new bases. Victory depends on your team’s ability to control vital worlds while crushing your foes in battle!

The Corellian Conflict also introduces twelve objectives that expand upon the challenges presented by the objectives in the Core Set. Finally, as you pursue these new paths to victory, you can consider the strategic advantages offered by sixteen unique squadron cards, all of which allow you to field legendary pilots and squadrons of the Galactic Civil War in combat!

This first version of Grandmama's Game of Riddles was one of a series of six Grandmama games issued by McLoughlin Bros. in the 1860s.

The game comes in a small wooden box and includes 120 numbered small cards, each with a riddle question. Any number may play, with one player taking on the role of "Preceptor" (holding the rule book) and the others as "Scholars".

The rules call for at least six cards to be dealt face down to each Scholar. Each Scholar in turn picks up one of his cards. If he answers correctly, he holds it. Otherwise, the opportunity to answer moves round the table clockwise, with the first Scholar to answer the riddle correctly keeping the card. Scholar with the most cards at the end of the game wins.

This undated party game was H.P. Gibson's first entry in the field of picture-puzzle party games. Unlike similar games produced by Universal and Party Games Publishers, which usually used decks of 18 cards with single images, the Gibson Series 1 puts 2-3 drawings on each of its 12 cards, for a total of 33 images.

Assuming 4, 8 or 12 players, the cards are then dealt out evenly to players, who get 1.5 minutes to guess the answers on their card(s). When time is up, they pass their cards to the players next to them. Once the cards have made the rounds, the player with the most correct answers wins.

In addition, this title uses the backs of the puzzle cards to offer a second game, Pennies. Each card has 12 clues starting "This PENNY..." and providing a blank where the answer is a word starting with the letters P-E-N. For example, the answer to "This PENNY gets in" is "PENetrate".

boardgame: Animal Antics or Where's My Dad
description:
This party game consists of 12 perforated sheets each of which breaks into a "Mr.", "Mrs." and "Baby" of an animal, for a total of 48 possible families.

The game is played like musical chairs and must be played by multiples of three players. The appropriate number of family member cards are distributed randomly to the players. Music starts, and players rapidly pass cards amongst one another. When the music stops, players must call out and rush to get together with the other two members of their family.

Last one to get to form a group on a chair or on the floor is eliminated. The game continues until a showdown round between the last six players, where they scramble for the available cards on the floor and assemble immediately.

Description from the designer:

Feathers & Wax is a solo print & play game using a double sided letter size game board, a standard deck of playing cards and two tokens.  This game is an entry in the 2016 Solitaire Print 'n Play contest.

Daedalus and his son Icarus are imprisoned on the edge of the sea. To escape, Daedalus is searching the labyrinth for feathers and wax to build wings for him and his son so that they can fly to safety.

Once Daedalus has collected his material, he builds two sets of wings. Then, he and his son leap from the tower and attempt to fly to safety. If they get too close to the sun, the wax melts, and some feathers are lost. If they get too close to the sea, some feathers get wet and weighed down and must be shed to keep flying.

If both Daedalus and Icarus can reach the safety of the far off island, they are victorious.

boardgame: Fallen Land: A Journey into Darkness
description:
1st expansion for Fallen Land. 
Now you can play solo or up to 6 players. 
108 New cards add 20% to the core set.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
The world's first tabletop novella!

Way out West, in the coldest part of the Wintery Woods lived the ghosts of songs he never played anymore.

Things must be remembered. Ghosts must never fade. Songs will keep you alive, now that the world has ended...

Welcome to Spell Saga ~ realmwalker ~ the world's first tabletop novella! This is a one player solo-style game with magical items, mysterious party members, & dangerous battles. There is no grinding for levels, as the game allows you to grow by exploring.

The ending of any realmwalker is always the same, but how you get there--and IF you get there, is up to you.

Each turn, you get a few cards in your hand, as well as some on the action pile on the table. One of the major finds can be story cards, that move the story (and the game) along. This will also trigger some of the cards (including enemies) in the action pile! You can perform different actions by moving your adventurers to different places - once you have discovered them! The story cards will (eventually) tell you how you can win the game.

This undated but early baseball game by Milton Bradley is a spin-and-move affair. The two players race from home plate around the bases and back to home on a field glued to the box bottom.

There are 8 spaces on each side of the diamond plus the squares for the bases. Each side has a red circle, which causes a player landing there to lose a turn. The three bases and home plate must be reached by exact spin.

Using a 4-space spinner, players must spin a 3 to start the game, but go directly to first base if they spin 4 three times before getting a 3.

There also are four hazard spaces on each track. The first, right after 2nd base, means a player is out (and starts over). The second and third, on the final leg, send the player back to third base. And the heartbreaking fourth, on the last space before winning, means the player is out at home.

Table Air Combat is a fast, simple air combat game that can be played on any flat surface. Each ruleset is a self-contained game including cut-out miniatures of one aircraft and a set of rules. All you need are some six-sided dice and some coins to provide weight for the aircraft counters.  Everything else can be printed from this ruleset.

Each player has a flight of four fighters, represented by two counters.  Aircraft counters move by means of curved “performance rulers” that represent the aircraft’s historical cornering ability and maximum speed.

TAC is designed for portability;  The entire game can fit in a plastic bag.  Even the largest battles can take place on a fast-food restaurant table.

The P-40 was used by several Allied nations during WWII and fought in Europe, North Africa, Asia, and the Pacific theaters.   Although less maneuverable than other fighters, the P-40 was fast, rugged, and well-armed.

This set is a self-contained rulebook that includes miniatures for the P-40E.  Miniatures are included for the AVG “Flying Tigers”, United States Air Force, Royal Air Force, Royal Australian Air Force, and Soviet Military Air Forces.

This turn of the 20th century educational game has a deck of 48 cards. Each card has between one and four geographical features listed, including continents, countries, rivers, mountains, and cities. Under each feature name is a list of questions to which that feature is the answer.

Cards are dealt out evenly among the players. The first player chooses a question on one of his cards and asks the player to his left for an answer. If the player cannot answer, he gives up a card to the questioner, who sets it aside. The questioner then moves to the next player with the same or a different question with the same answer and continues until someone answers correctly. If no one answers, the questioner sets it aside for himself and chooses a new card. When a question is answered correctly, the questioner surrenders a card to the answerer and the questioner's turn ends.

All cards won are set aside and out of the game. When all of the cards have been claimed, the player with the most cards wins.

Alex G. Malloy in American Games: Comprehensive Collector's Guid (1999) dates this game as circa 1890s, but the deck includes two cards with questions about the "murderous revolt against Christian civilization" and the capture of Pekin by "American and allied troops", both in 1900. Another card refers to Cuba as a country rather than a Spanish colony, and the game includes an ad for the "fine new games" of The Comical Game of Who (1899) and Sir Hinkle Funnyduster (1903), with the latter providing the likely publication date of this game as well.

Chicken is a quick, press-your-luck party game for 4-16 players. Based on hot-rod drag-racing, players race towards the edge of a cliff to see who can get closest without going over. Each round the road cards are placed face down. Players then places their marker where they would like to stop. Road cards are flipped over one at a time, with each road card being worth one or two points and the cliff is worth three. Players earn all points from the beginning to where they stop, unless they go over the cliff and forfeit all points gained for the round. The first player to 30 points wins.

Description from the publisher:

Crazy Robot Factory is an abstract/puzzle solving/token placement solitaire/co-op game where you are the owner of a new robot parts factory and you've arrived to find out that the robots who setup the main factory floor must have had a glitch and placed the robot assemblers and conveyor belts randomly across the factory floor. What's worse is you have a huge order that is going to start processing and your factory isn't ready!

So to try and get everything working again you must quickly program your factory Robots to help move the equipment into their correct positions. You will have to do this while you use your Factory Assembly Arms to bring in new equipment onto the factory floor. Don't take too long rearranging the conveyor belt because once the START conveyor belts begins moving you must make sure the conveyor belts have a continuous path or the widgets will drop to the factory floor and end up destroyed.

This is a paper and pencil party game designed to be played in a large gathering, generally in tables of four with players on opposite sides acting as partners. The objective is to draw the creature shown on the score sheet. Two creatures are available, a giraffe and a penguin. The game includes 25 player sheets, each with squares for 12 drawings.

Players take turns rolling a die, with each number corresponding to a particular part of the creature's body. A roll of 6 is needed to start a drawing, and when one is rolled, both partners can draw the body. A five must be rolled next, and again allows both partners to draw the head. After that, each partner rolls only for his own drawing.

The first player to complete a drawing yells "Gooffy" and the players then tally their individual scores (one point per body part). The two highest scorers move to the next table for another round, and the game continues for as many as 12 rounds. The player with the highest total points at the end of the game wins.

The giraffe needs a tail (4), a neck (3), two ears and an eye (2) and four legs (1). The penguin needs a beak (4), one eye (3), two feet (2) and two flippers (1). Each roll only allows one of the corresponding parts to be added.

This Sixties race game from Whitman has a very unusual mechanism for determining how far a player can move. This is an 8" long blue plastic paddle, shaped like a rounded diamond with handle. A small red ball is attached to the end of the paddle by a 4" string. There are six holes in the paddle, five in an arc at the 4" limit of the string, and one in the center near the nose.

Each player swings the paddle in an effort to get the ball through one of the holes. The easiest, in the center, is a move of 1. Values increase toward the edges, with 2 and 3 partway and 4 and 5 on the edges. The hole near the nose is worth two free turns.

While the board box shows a map of the United States, the board itself is an abstract grid allowing orthogonal and diagonal moves. Black dots are obstacles that force a player to stop and then go around. Landing on a dot of your own color allows a free move in any direction. Landing on another player's color allows that player to move you one in a direction of his choice. First to cross the board wins.

All aboard! If you want to set off on your journey, you have to find 8 passengers and then the driver, quickly! Will you be the first to fill your train?
In this pick up and deliver game, players will try to fulfill their 6 wagons with corresponding passengers by picking them up along train tracks.
On their turn, a player picks a new passenger randomly from the passenger bag and puts it on the board. Then this player moves their train along train track by choosing one color track. They can ride along this track and pick up a passenger if corresponding to a needed color. Or They can stop in a train station and change track on their next turn.
First player to collect 6 passenger win the game.

In Club Town, all players are town planners helping to build up a town into a thriving metropolis. Players would be competing to gain favorable building contracts, build up the city, making sound financial investments as well as making financial contribution to impress the town council and mayor.

Club Town is a unique town building game that uses trick taking as its main action selection mechanic. Heavily inspired by Hearts and Diamonds (designer: Mike Fitzgerald), each suit is will have its associated action and players will need to take stock of what has been dealt to them, plan their strategy and out-think their opponent to be the most prestigious firm in town.

In a trick, each player would play a card following the lead and the suit would determine the action a player may take and the value determines how much money or investment opportunities may be gained or spent during the action: Spades to build buildings, Hearts to gain investor prospects, Clubs to earn income and Diamonds to invest in buildings. Being the leader would allow you control what everyone may do, but watch out as someone might pair their card with Influence (Joker) card to trump yours instead.

The game ends in four rounds running the whole poker deck twice and the prestige would be gained from buildings, their own investments in buildings and having the majority in favors in the Town Hall. May you be the one who outsmarts your friends and gain the highest prestige to be declared the prime developer in town!

Taschkent Erweiterung is an expansion for Taschkent.

The expansion consists of two parts:

The statues: 
The famous statues of Tashkent were destroyed in a big storm. Players who pay homage to the trade gods by rebuilding the statues, get an additional reward.

The followers: 
Sultan, tribal elder, harem leader, trader and nomad – these new followers find their way into Tashkent and offer new ways to get victory points. At the beginning each player is assigned an exclusive follower, another one can be used by all players.

Description from the publisher:

Beware! This game will drive you mental! The name of this game is using your brain. Answer lateral thinking questions that challenge your own brain whilst trying to beat your friends. Players try to make their way across a board by answering lateral thinking puzzles correctly. Or, if you're the curious type, just browse through the fascinating conumdrums solo! A great family game for 2 to 4 players.

boardgame: Plenty of Fish in the Sea?: Delving Deeper
description:
With deeper water you can increase the niches your species can exploit. Seek out Sponges, take refuge in the wrecks, hide in Sea grass or co habit with a protective species. Whilst things seem to be getting easier you will also have to cope with a Fishing boat and Oil pollution, plus deeper means less oxygen and harder living standards.

Adds extra fish, tiles, new game effects for tiles and new predator options to Plenty of Fish in the Sea?

-description from the publisher

Deutschland's Kolonien-Spiel ("Germany's Colonies Game") is a game published in Germany around 1890, in which players move tokens around a map to visit different German colonies, paying to see the sites and learn about these places.

It's a roll and move racing game - first player to reach the China space wins.

Join your friends from Disney Frozen in a clever game of hide-and-seek! One player hides the included Olaf figuring and other players ask questions and use deduction to figure out where Olaf could be. Is he in a room with a tub? Is he in a room with a pillow? Each question gets a HOTTER or COLDER answer. When you get the chance to go look, see if you can find Olaf before the time runs out!.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: It's the Tipsy, Topsy Numbers Game that includes three levels of play: number recognition, counting and addition! Pick a card and practice counting or adding the puppies and checking your answer by balancing numbers and puppies on the adorable scale! It's an entertaining way to introduce children to math and get them excited about learning! For 1 to 4 players, ages 3 and up. Children also have fun weighing other household items...how many puppies does this or that toy weigh? And the puppies are toys themselves, too! It's tons of fun with STEM! Colors will vary.

An expansion for Dorasure that contains three new characters: Archer, Bishop and Jester.

Archer: can sacrifice movement dice for resources; co-operate on quests from long range; and utilise two dice in Team Combos. Her treasure item allows her to change an ally’s Climactic Battle result to a Team Combo.

Bishop: has the same resource as the base game’s Paladin. She can heal allies as an action; gain extra XP when completing quests with “magical” affinity; and add her Knowledge roll to an ally’s Combat quest attempt. Her treasure item allows her to use her Knowledge roll instead of a Combat roll in the Climactic Battle (effectively forfeiting the use of the Anti-Dragon Weapons).

Jester: can gain resources through Peak Roll fumbles and criticals. The Jester can also use two dice when determining the outcome of a Roaming Dragon action and select the preferred result; and uncover a single quest/reward tile pairing upon reaching level one. Once per game, the Jester’s treasure item lets you flip the results of a Peak Roll in the Climactic Battle phase (potentially converting a disaster into a sure hit against the dragon).
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
A promotional card given out during the 2016 Watch It Played Indiegogo campaign.

This promo introduces a new Encounter Card (#38) that you can use in the game. The card  shows a mech next to a man fishing in a lake.

The card offers the following options:

     Quietly watch the fisherman from across the lake: Gain 2 popularity.
     Broker a deal for the old mech: Pay $4 to deploy 1 mech.
     Smile as you push the mech into the lake: Pay 2 popularity to gain 4 food.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
It’s The Greedy Sheep game… when he bleats, he wants to eat!

This little sheep loves carrots and wants to gobble them all up. Push his head down to take a bite and hear him much and chomp!

Feed him carrots, until he bleats, then it’s time to pass to the next player for their turn.

Keep feeding and passing, but watch out, when his tummy’s full The Greedy Sheep finally goes BAA BAA BOOM and his fleece flies off!

The winner is the player with the least carrots remaining.

boardgame: Dungeon Saga: The Warlord of Galahir
description:
This quest pack introduces an all-new storyline, challenging adventures and exciting new components to add to your Dungeon Saga collection.

In The Warlord of Galahir, join the battle to free the imprisoned merchant Pietor Grube from the clutches of foul Orcs, recover the treasures of Sarennia and destroy the tyrannical warlord despoiling the once idyllic forests of Galahir. Do you have what it takes to restore peace and civility to the good people of Penn-Scarr?

Lead Four New Heroes! Kapoka the Gladewalker Druid and the Salamander named Hrrath enter the fray, along with Thesilar the ranger and fighter Guraf

Invade the Orc Lair! Journey through the dank, subterranean habitat of the green-skins as you hunt down the menace bringing terror to the land

Vanquish the Horde! Wade through legions of Orcs, Goblins, Mawbeasts and other beings of brutal ferocity in a visceral fight for survival

Confront Thrundak Skullsplitter! Renowned even among his own kin for his extreme cruelty, this furiously violent Orc leaves nothing but rivers of blood in his wake.

Show the savages no mercy.

In the base Dungeon Saga one player takes the part of the evil dungeon overlord with the forces of evil at his command, and the others divide the mighty heroes between them. Each hero has strengths and weaknesses and teamwork is the key: The mighty barbarian can slaughter many foes at a time, yet he is lightly armoured and vulnerable to strong enemies. The Dwarf, on the other hand, is steadier as his thick armour can resist most attacks. The Elf is not as skilled a fighter as either of these, instead preferring to stay at a distance where her finely-honed archery skills can best be used. Finally, there is the wizard -the others may laugh at his lack of fighting skill, yet it is often his magic that carries the day. If the others can keep him alive, that is.

Soqquadro Outdoor is the outdoor version of Soqquadro.

Just like the basic version of Soqquadro, you draw a card from a deck and search for an object that satisfies the request on the card, all in real time. When you find the object, place it on the table and draw another card. 
Soqquadro Outdoor is simpler than the original game, since it comes with only one deck. There are no pawns nor score boards: the game ends when a player finds a certain amount of objects (depending on the number of players). All the cards have simple requirements and are designed specifically for outdoor playing.
Soqquadro Outdoor is perfect for children and families.

This is a variant of Jenga, published without rules, but including a wooden six sided dice.

Each building block is either blank, features the WWF panda logo or is painted with the fur patterns of either giraffe, cheetah or zebra. The dice has coresponding sides to the blocks and therefore supposingly is to be rolled to determine, which type of block is legal to take away.

Contains 48 wooden blocks and 1 dice.

User summary
HACKER is a 2 player game consisting of digit cards numbered 1 through 9. Using these cards, battle with your opponent to produce the larger number in order to win. (A card’s number can be changed based on the “Big” and “Small” numbers printed at the top and bottom of each card. For example: 6⇔9, 1⇔3)
Each player has a deck composed of the same 9 cards from which they randomly discard 2 cards. The game starts with each player holding the 7 remaining cards as their hand.

The first player chooses the “Winning Conditions” (BIG WIN/SMALL WIN) and places one card face down. The second player then analyzes why the first player chose those certain winning conditions to guess the player’s card in order to place a stronger card face up.

When both cards have been placed on the table, the first player turns their card face up and the card that better fulfills the winning conditions wins the round.

Switching the role of first and second player each round, continue playing for seven rounds. The first player to accumulate four winning rounds wins the game.

Publisher's summary
「HACKER」は１～９のデジタル表記の数字の書かれたカードを使って、数字の大きさ比べで対戦する２人用ゲームです。（数字は上下を変えることで違う数字として使えます。例：6⇔9、1⇔3）
２人は同じ構成のカード９枚を持ち、その中からランダムに２枚除外した７枚を使って、７回対戦を行ないます。

先手が「勝利条件（BIG WIN / SMALL WIN)」を選んで１枚伏せて置きます、後手は「なぜ先手はその勝利条件を選んだのか」ということから先手の「数字（カード）」を推測し、それに勝てる「数字（カード）」を表向きに出します。
両者のカードがそろったら、先手のカードを表向きにして、今回の勝利条件に合ったカードを出した人が１勝します。

先手・後手を交互に行ない、７試合中の内で先に４勝した人がゲームの勝者になります。

Desolate Front is an alternate World War I card game in which myth and magic fight side by side with science and technology.

Players use soldiers, officers, heroes and other items, such as The Master of the Hunt and Frankenstein's Creature, to try to take control of the battlefield. Use the latest equipment, stalwart allies, and powerful magic to vanquish your opponent's characters and secure key areas in your advance to victory.

A cult has turned to violence to push its agenda and to garner more attention. Many in the cult are as much victims as they are culprits. How will you handle the situation when the Abductors are also the Hostages? Find out in this thrilling pack.

This Abductor pack is a 16 card expansion compatible with both Hostage Negotiator and Hostage Negotiator: Crime Wave.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Clyde Seaver's life is in shambles. His wife left him and in fear of losing his 2 children he's kidnapped them with the help of his loyal sister, Tina. She has the kids hidden in Becker Swamp and you have to negotiate with the suicidal Clyde and buy your team time to find the kids.

This Abductor pack is a 15 card expansion compatible with both Hostage Negotiator and Hostage Negotiator: Crime Wave.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Valerie Stone was another negotiator who was constantly competing with you, but never garnered the accolades or had the level of success that you did. Ultimately it became too much for her and she's decided the only way to prove she is better than you is to force you to negotiate with her... and she knows just how to accomplish that.

This Abductor pack is a 16 card expansion compatible with both Hostage Negotiator and Hostage Negotiator: Crime Wave.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
Deck Quest (DQ) is an open-ended tabletop RPG condensed into four decks!

Much of DQ's gameplay relies on the exciting, funny, and creative power of the human mind to come up with solutions and plans for various situations.

DQ takes place in a fantasy world with dragons, paladins, mages, and other similar aspects--perhaps even the famous DQ blizzard!

The goal of the game is to complete whatever quests the Game Master presents to the players. The players may decide to work in teams and/or individually--the game is completed when the given quest or quests are all accomplished.

The White Whale promo pack includes four new tiles that may be placed in the whaling bag. Each tile has a different effect when drawn and selected:

White Whale - This whale is wily and dangerous; it has a big payout, but it will cost you a ship. 
Ambergris - A rare, waxlike substance produced by whales and used in perfuming, which provides $8 upon return to port. 
Castaway - A lone survivor found on an island; he provides an extra action. 
Blue Whale - A whale so big that it fills the entire boat, and the art barely fits on the tile.

Note: This promo (provided to Kickstarter backers) was packaged separately and is not included with the Rising Tide expansion.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Activity 25th anniversary edition is special edition to the 25th anniversary of the hit among the party-games. As before, three difficulty levels and three ways of the explanation, but there are new words. The new design will make the game even more active!

This is a party game from Piatnik played in 2-4 teams with at least 2 players each. The teams' meeples move over the board, the goal is to reach the finish. Depending on the sort of field they are currently standing at, one player has to act (pantomime) or to describe or to paint a phrase given on a card and the other members of his team have to find out what that should be. If they manage to do so, their meeple moves forward.
There are several editions and expansions for this game.

Description from the publisher:

What is  Everdark ?

Everdark is a 2-4 player semi co-operative rotating maze strategy game where teamwork is encouraged but by no means guaranteed. Players are given events to complete that are located around the city board. They have to navigate their way to these locations by moving their character and twisting the rings on the board. Any areas that are not defended succumb to darkness and the maze becomes harder to navigate

It is a mid-level game taking around 60-90 minutes to play. The length of the game can depend on the rule variation used by players. The key reasoning for that is that the use of the timer (whilst recommended) is not compulsory. This, among other rules, allows flexibility among players to play Everdark in a way of their own choosing.

Description from the publisher:

The Persians have sacked Athens and the citizens look to their leaders to rebuild their city. As a demagogue of one of the ten tribes, it is your duty to increase both their power and your influence over them. The more powerful the people is, the more new citizens will come to Athens, but they will only come to the most influential demagogue.

The player who first plays all of his or her citizens to Athens wins.

Demokratia is played in rounds divided into five phases:

1) Rhetorics: Argue, negotiate and bribe for your cause.
2) Ekklesia (Assembly): Play secret votes.
3) Archon: Play citizens to the city of Athens OR to
the Boule (Council) OR to a Monument/Rhetor.
4) Phylai: Reveal votes and distribute new citizens.
5) Ostracism: Play citizens to temporarily exile a demagogue.

The players take turns to play their citizens to Athens. During each round, the power and the influence will rise and fall.


     The colors of the cast votes and the erected buildings in Athens determine the tribe power, which in turn determine how many new citizens will come to Athens.
     The citizens in the Boule determine the tribe influence, which in turn determine the share of the new citizens each player will get.
     The players may also claim Monuments or Rhetors, each with a unique ability to influence the democratic game.


To win Demokratia, you must not only favor your own tribes but also maintain your influence over them so that no other player grow more influential and gets the power of your effort.

Demokratia is a double majority game, where you must be the strongest in the strongest tribe. In addition, the ostracism mechanism is a double catch-up mechanism: the second player can spend a citizen to exile the first player, giving the third and the fourth player a chance to catch up.

Description from the publisher:

Politeia takes the players back to the Persian Wars. Faced with the threat of a Persian invasion, the players must cooperate to defend their civilization. However, the competition for wealth and resources is fierce, as simulated by an inventive action mechanism, and just like the Ancient Greeks, the players are likely to turn their weapons against each other instead.

The different player actions add talents from the stock to the hand and turn those talents into citizens on the board, engaging in peaceful trading or aggressive warfare. Once a player run out of stock, the player with the most citizens left on the board wins.

The game board consists of tiles representing city-states, colonies and diplomatic positions. The player markers represent either talents or citizens and control of the board is determined by area majority.

The players take turns to select actions from a common mancala style action board. They pick up the action markers in one action space and drop them one by one in the spaces of the actions they wish to take, paying a cost equal to the number of action markers already in there.

The actions can broadly be divided into four groups:

1) Acquire talents, either directly through taxes and levies or indirectly through import of resources that can later be exchanged for talents.
2) Invest talents in buildings, providing action benefits, or diplomacy, providing war benefits.
3) Turn talents into citizens, either to consolidate existing city-states or to expand to new city-states.
4) Use citizens to attack other players or defend against the Persians.

The more citizens a player gets to the board, the more talents he or she may generate but the greater the risk if the Persians invade.

The unique action mechanism allows the player to optimize action combinations while at the same time blocking other players' actions by making them more expensive. This opens up several strategic paths, spanning from slow engine building to sudden seizures of area majorities. All game elements have a historical background, making Politeia a game of traditional Euro mechanisms linked by a strong theme.

Description from the publisher:

Lucca is an abstract game where you play tiles (houses) in groups (blocks) and stacks (towers) to dominate the board (city). The board size depends on the level you wish to play - the bigger the board, the deeper the game.

You control a block by controlling the tower of the block and you control the tower by having the top house of the tower. On a standard 8x8 board, you win if, at the start of your turn, your tower is the highest and has a height of at least 8 houses. Alternatively you win by defeating other towers and taking at least 4 sets of prisoners.

Take turns to place one of your own tiles in an empty square or any number of tiles of any color beneath already placed tiles to build a tower.

There may only be one tower in a block and its colors must correspond to the colors of the block, i.e. two black tiles in the block allows two black tiles in the tower.
 
When two blocks grow together, an equal amount of tiles is removed from the two towers, leaving only the strongest tower but potentially with a new controlling color.

The challenge of Lucca is to time the tower building and removal so that your own color prevails in the end.

The unique mechanism of Lucca is the four dimensional thinking where not only the block area and the tower height must be taken into account but also the timing of the removal. All this is accomplished in a pure and elegant setting where luck plays no part.

In Save the Cupcake, one of you has a cupcake, while the other one desires to crush the cupcake in the most epic way possible — by rolling a ball down a hill to run it over.

The Defender of Cupcakes will hide the cupcake in one of many possible locations at the bottom of the hill. The Crusher of Cupcakes will roll four balls down the hill, through an elaborate network of fruit-themed pathways. The Defender and Crusher must cleverly use these pathways to meet their ultimate cupcake goals!
A 51-card expansion deck for Cul-De-Sac Conquest, featuring new adult-oriented (NSFW) cards.
This promo pack for Scythe includes encounter cards 33-36, all of which are included in every Kickstarter copy of Scythe (STM601-604) but NOT in any retail copy (STM600).
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
An Unofficial Cards Against Humanity expansion.

Includes 112 NEW cards (90 White cards & 22 Black cards)

Professionally printed Casino Quality Linen Finish cards, 100% compatible with Cards Against Humanity.

boardgame: Isle of Skye: From Chieftain to King – Kennerspiel des Jahres Promo Tile
description:
Promo Tile published on the occasion of the "Kennerspiel des Jahres" award 2016.

The tile shows 2 barrels of whisky (which will increase the income by 2 gold if connected to the player's castle via roads), and 1 cattle.

boardgame: The Pursuit of Happiness: Space Trip Promo Card
description:
Space Trip is an additional Group Project that can be added to the base game.

Available at Essen Spiel 2016, it was also included in the Kickstarter version of the game.

boardgame: Trek: World Travel Race by Land, Sea and Air
description:
This is a roll and move game across a map of the world divided into a grid 52 spaces wide (E-W) and 33 spaces deep (N-S). There are four start spaces, two on each of the east and west edges, and travellers (wooden triangles) must use a variety of vehicles (wooden cubes and cylinders) to get to the opposite edge of the board and on the other side of the equator.

Sea squares are blue, with some light blue squares that are impassable. Land squares are green, red, yellow or purple. There are eight modes of transportation, each restricted by color: cars and trains (green); aeroplanes (green, red and cross-hatched yellow, blue or purple; horses (red); camels (yellow), porters and pack animals (purple); and vessels (blue). These are scattered across the map on designated squares at the start of the game.

Players throw three dice on their turn. The red die and one white die are normal D6, the second white die is directional (N,S,E,W, and two blanks). Travellers must be on some form of transportation to move, and the white dice determine how many squares and in which direction (blanks are player's choice). The red die is used to move empty vehicles to where the traveller can intercept them and change modes. Players must move the full amount unless it brings them to a terrain border, and must change modes if they encounter an unoccupied vehicle.

Although the game is undated, it appears to be a 1930s design, since aeroplanes can only move short distances across water (Mediterranean, Caribbean, SE Asia) and not across oceans. Also, as common in the 1930s, the game comes in a small box with a separate board.

A promotional card given out during the 2016 Watch It Played Indiegogo campaign.

This promo introduces a new gun themed Spoils Card that you can use in the game.

This is a party game with a Mafia theme that combines play-acting with a Prohibition-era trivia game. The box includes eight character cards, a deck of 48 trivia question and answer cards, party invitations and envelopes with "an offer you can't refuse", menu cards, a background booklet that includes recipes for the menu dishes and even a CD of appropriate music. The dinner allegedly takes place at Scarpato's Restaurant on Coney Island in 1931.

The character cards include Al Capone and his ardently churchgoing wife Mae, along with Meyer Lansky and his new, skinny, pregnant and naïve Italian bride Anna Citron. Each of these cards includes characteristics, background information and suggested topics to guide play: Mae is violently allergic to dust, while Anna speaks only broken English and thinks words like "mob" and "burial" are management talk from her consultant husband.

Like many party games, the primary goal is mutual amusement and there are no specific rules for winning. Clearly the person answering the most trivia questions correctly could be declared a winner of that activity, and there might be a vote on best character acting.

This is an undated space travel game from Belgium that combines roll-and-move with resource management. The board covers the solar system, and the goal is to accumulate the most money primarily by being the first to land on planets. Landing bonuses range from 100 million for the moon to 1,000 million for Pluto, and there are game end bonuses for last planet, each planet card and most planet cards.

The standard red interplanetary track includes hazards that can bump players onto a useless green track. The red track leads to yellow orbital tracks around each planet, which in turn link to white landing tracks. Players must try to hit cross-over and landing points precisely, and can be damaged or even killed if they land too fast.

On each turn, players declare their acceleration and pay the appropriate amount of fuel, from 1 tonne for 1D6 to 32 tonnes for 6D6. Players may instead spend 5 tonnes for a retro thrust (1D6-3) or 20 tonnes for a super-retro (1D6 in reverse). Players may also spend money for help from an "electronic brain" that allows movement of exactly 1 in either direction for 50 million up to 4 for 400 million.

Players can buy fuel cheaply while on a planet or at double price at any time. It also can be had for free by landing on a space station (25t) and by giving up a turn while on a planet or station (10t). Players can borrow money from the bank if necessary to buy fuel.

The rules are in Flemish and French, and while the game is undated, the box graphic looks a lot like the space ship from the Tintin graphic novels.

Description from the publisher:

Beyond the Mist is a two-player card game, for players of all ages, that takes about 10 to 15 minutes to play.
Each player chooses their hand of cards and plays according to their own strategy in an ever-changing board. By choosing among a range of mythical adventurers the players pick those that can help them populate the lands and score the most points for themselves.
However, each player carries the support of a faction that wishes to influence their actions and lead their opponent to ruin.
And once a victor is declared, the heroes carry on to a next expanse of land, forging new alliances and trying to bring prestige for themselves and their lords!

This undated card game has a deck of 40 cards with a motor vehicle theme. There are four suits (red, blue, green, yellow) and most of the cards have illustrations of cars, trucks, motorcycles or buses. A few cards (different in each suit) have hazards or stop signs.

The player to the left of the dealer starts by playing his lowest card of any suit. Whoever has the next highest puts it on top, with play continuing until someone plays a stop sign card or until no one can follow. Then the next player starts a different colored suit (missing a turn if he has no other color). Winner is the first to get rid of all his cards.

boardgame: The Lord of the Rings: The Card Game – The Siege of Annuminas
description:
Description from the publisher:

In The Siege of Annuminas, the ravaging hordes of Angmar are marching on Annuminas, and it’s up to the players to defend the city in an adventure designed for 1-12 players. This unique scenario can be played in two different game modes: Standard Game Mode or Epic Multiplayer Mode. Standard Game Mode requires only 1 copy of The Siege of Annuminas and supports 1-4 players like every other The Lord of the Rings LCG scenario. But Epic Multiplayer Mode requires three copies of The Siege of Annuminas and supports 3-12 players!
family: Collectible: Living Card Game (Fantasy Flight Games)
Game: The Lord of the Rings – The Card Game (LCG)

boardgame: The New Game of Blockade Runner
description:
This is an early 20th Century British game loosely based on the 1904-05 Russo-Japanese war and in particular the Japanese blockade of Port Arthur in the early stages of the war.

The label on the back of the board shows ships of the period along with the Russian and Japanese flags, and has the subtitle: "A Game of Skill Without Intricate Rules".

The board does not make these rules clear, but there is a Start position at the southern end of the Yellow Sea and a web of red lines and dots leading to ports in China, Manchuria and Korea. Seven ports have their names and the word "HOME" in a white box, and there is an additional "HOME" port for Manchuria as a whole.

The board bears the words "Design Registered" and "British Manufacture" but no date, publisher, copyright or patent claim. Given that the Strait of Korea is at the east edge of the map (leaving out the neighboring Tsushima Strait where the climactic naval battle of the war occurred, this game may well have been published in 1904, during the first half of the war.
This is a promotional battlefield for Kenjin. To win this battlefield, you must have a lower strength than your opponent.
This is a promotional captain board for Sea of Clouds.
This is a promotional captain board for Sea of Clouds.

Ultra 7 is a fast, fun card game with plenty of twists and turns. After being dealt 7 cards each, players take turns playing cards face up onto one of two play piles. The card being played must match the top card in terms of color, number, or suit, and the first player to rid their hand of cards wins. However, the deck also contains an abundance of special cards. For example, the Flip card requires a coin toss, the results of which determine who must draw two cards. The Trade card provides the option of trading hands with another player. The Grab card is a mad grab for spoons (one less than the number of players), with the empty-handed player required to draw two cards. The dreaded Spoiler card can be a major roadblock to winning. The unpredictability these cards bring to Ultra 7 lend it an exciting playfulness that make for a rip-roaring good time.

The goal is to get rid of all your cards before the other players.  Match the color, number, suit, or name of the card on one of the play piles in order to play a card down.

Watch out for the special cards... you may have to pick up a spoon, swap spots with another player, swap hands, pass cards, or get the dreaded SPOILER card.

This 1964 logic game has a mechanic similar to the later Mastermind, but is played with cards and words. There is a deck of 26 Blackout cards, each with a six-letter word on one side, and an irregular inkblot on the other side. There is a second deck of 70 game cards, each of which has a pattern of holes punched through the card.

On each turn, a player takes one of the blackout word cards and places the pattern side underneath the game card. The number of black dots showing is the number of letters in that word card that appear in the game card's word.

Using a scorecard that lists each of the 26 word cards, the player must try one word card after another to determine the six letters represented by his game card, and then work out the right order for the actual word.

The rules provide 5 variants for two players, two variants for party fun, and one for solitaire. In head-to-head play, the player who takes the most cards to succeed loses points equal to his number of turns minus the number taken by his opponent, plus a flat penalty of 3 points for losing. First player to 50 points loses the game.

This is a silly spin-and-move game where schoolboy players and their catapults must try to get from top of the First Form (average IQ) to bottom of the Fourth Form (Half-Witted) while collecting as many Black Marks, Detentions, and Whack-Os as possible.

Players may have their catapults confiscated (and must pay 3 points worth of cards to get them back in order to finish), and be delayed by being sent to Coventry, the Tuck Shop or the San. While the first player to finish gets 5 bonus points, victory is determined by number and quality of demerits, with each Whack-O worth 3 points, each Detention 2 and each Black Mark 1.

The game is based on a British comedy series that according to Wikipedia ran in black-and-white from 1956-60 and later in color from 1971-72. It featured comedy writer and actor Jimmy Edwards as "the drunken, gambling, devious, cane-swishing headmaster who tyrannised staff and children at Chiselbury public school". Series creators Frank Muir and Denis Norden are give a "by arrangement with" credit on the box cover.

While the game is undated, it features a black-and-white photo of Jimmy Edwards's face atop a cartoon body, placing it as a late 1950s publication.

boardgame: Perks: The Government House of Cards
description:
Description from the publisher:

Do you remember the scandal when Congress members had huge overdrafts in their checking accounts and were not paying it back?  Well this game lets you do it, too!  The goal of the game is to overdraw your checking amount by more than your opponent(s).

Set up the game by placing the numbered cards in 11 face down stacks according to the numbers printed on them (2-12).  The numbers corresponds to the following political job titles: 2 Vice President, 3  President for a Day, 4  Retiring Politician, 5  Cabinet Member, 6  Incumbent Politician, 7  House of Representatives, 8  Politician's Aid, 9  Diplomat, 10  Senator, 11  Political Appointee, and 12 White House Chief of Staff.

During their turn, players roll the dice and take a card matching the number.  The cards instruct the player to record a debit (subtraction) or a credit (addition) in their check register.

The game ends when a player has a full check register page - 20 items.  The winner with the highest overdraft wins!

Two Bible word games in one box.

Bible Spell-A-Gram:  Alphabet tiles are placed face down between the players.  Each player draws 20 tiles and takes turn spelling as many words as they can in 3 minutes.  They replenish tiles at the end of their turn.  First player to spell 10 words wins.

Bible Spell-Across:  Each player gets a set of alphabet tiles and takes turns placing words, crossword style, on a grid on the back of the box.

ELLIPSIS (…) is a party card game where players try to compare things that are not necessarily similar. In addition, players have to explain their comparisons in the form of some sort of punch line. For example, “parents are like boogers … always hanging around making you look like a fool” or “parents are like belts … supportive.” Here’s the catch, the key word revealed on the drawn card must be compared to a certain letter word. Rolling the 20 sided die will dictate the starting letter of a word. In the aforementioned example, the key word was “parents” and the letter “B” was rolled to come up with the words “boogers” and “belts”. Notice the dot dot dot (…) in the example? It’s called an ELLIPSIS. Each player will write their own witty, humorous, or even barbed punch line following the ELLIPSIS. Players earn points for guessing who said what.

This is a paper-and-pencil game, clearly a "beer and pretzels" game since it promotes the witbier (white beer) produced by the Belgian brewer Hoegaarden.

There is a deck of 124 cards, each of which has six words in both French and Flemish. There also are two pencils, a pad of square drawing sheets and a bag of blue and white chips.

As in charades but on paper, players are challenged to pick a word on their card and try to draw an image that will prompt the correct response from their partners or teams.

Description from the publisher:

Action and goal
You have 3 decks of cards. Terrorists, counter-terrorists and action cards(which help ter. or c.-t. to win battle but you can do not use action card if feel your person card is strong enough).
(1 vs 1) You can mix all cards and play so. Or you can play for any of 2 sides(terrorists or c.-t.).
The goal is to have any card(terrorists or c-t)by the end of the game.

Fun
This game have everything: the capture and release of the hostages, and defuzing bombs, monitoring, not a team playing, and even the invasion of zombies (coming zombies servers apparently). Game have ultimate funy combinations like - 3 meme royalflash kittys or Cheeter or Admin card. 
The game was originally planned on the card field, but in principle has been made for more user-friendly format of the game.
Game developed with well-known CS players with experience of 10 years of playing. 
Skins special forces of many countries.
Playing with humor.
It is interesting to play a lot of micro rounds. When you become pro it will be interesting to play against 2 or three players.
In plans to hold tournaments in the discipline of CS cards.
So you can already start training.

Energie-Spar-Spiel or the Energy Saving Game is a simple roll-and-move affair published in 1981 by the German subsidiary of electrical products maker GTE Sylvania.

The game board has a slightly spiral track of 80 spaces. There are 16 spaces along the way marked in red, each of which has a bonus or penalty linked to a good or bad energy use behaviour. Each situation and its consequences are listed in an accompanying booklet.

The board is vividly illustrated by examples of the red dot situations, from drying dishes by hand and installing fluourescent bulbs to a car slamming on the brakes at a stoplight and a topless woman "talking to Susie on the phone for two hours" while her hot bath goes cold.

The game title is in German, but it includes an English rule book.

A fast party game in which a player must read an answer among the available 300 and you must be the quickest to find a question.
But you must follow the game rules to do it!

HOW TO FORMULATE A QUESTION:
To be valid, a question must fulfill the following requests:
1: contain at least one of the four random words on the table
2: follow the special rule of the round, if there is one (example: "in rhyme" or "as in a theatrical performance"
3: be new: you are not allowed to repeat questions already made
4: of course, it can have the current answer, as its answer!

L’idea di base del gioco è molto semplice: il giocatore di turno legge una delle 300 risposte scritte sulle carte e il più veloce fra gli altri a fargli la domanda giusta vince la manche!
Ma ovviamente non è tutto qui: ci sono parole obbligatorie da usare, termini troppo ovvi da evitare e 20 bizzarre modalità di gioco da rispettare.

Description from the designer:

Today I began full-on work on my next CounterFact magazine design, 1938: What If? which presumes the Czechoslovakians refused to accept the Munich Dictat. Longer story short: On or around 1 October, the Germans put "Operation Green" into effect and war starts. The sides are: the Czechoslovakians and Soviets vs. the Germans, Poles and Hungarians.

I believe my treatment is original here in that, unlike all the games I'm familiar with on this topic, I'm presenting an examination of the larger picture here. That is, rather than the map just covering Bohemia/Moravia and environs, this one shows (at 20 miles per hex) all of Czechoslovakia and Poland, along with the German, Hungarian, Romanian, Lithuanian and Soviet border lands thereunto appertaining.

The turns equal three days each, and the game has a maximum of 10 turns. Units of maneuver are two-step corps all around, except the Germans' 7th Flieger (proto)-division gets its own counter (and a likely suicide mission).

I'm using an adaptation of my "Zhukov's War" system (which doesn't mean anything to any of you yet, because that parent design itself hasn't yet appeared.

At the system's core, there are 10 chits, one for each maneuverable nationality's movement phase and another for each one's combat phase (the Lithuanians, if they get dragged in, just defend their capital; so there's no chit for them).

All but the two German chits go into a pool at the start of every turn. The two German chits are held out. The German player may interpose either of those chits at any time in the turn (together or separately, in either order), but otherwise the phase sequence is determined by blind draw (and, in this particular case, Turn 1 will always start with the German combat phase, thereby kicking off the overall war).

That system neatly shows the advantages of having the advantage in C3.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

boardgame: Monarch: Hungry Tarasque and Regal Peacock
description:
Description from the publisher:

Who doesn't love beasts? The mini expansion Monarch: Hungry Tarasque and Regal Peacock adds two new beasts to be shuffled into the Monarch deck: the Hungry Tarasque and Regal Peacock.

This expansion works best with the second printing of Monarch.

This is a roll and move game with two different playing pieces: pegs and circles (otherwise known as bolts and washers). There are three of each in three colors (black, white and orange. There also is a set of 40 Oh Peg It cards, each with a familiar saying that swaps out a word and replaces it with "peg", and instructions for gaining or losing spaces or points. There is a set of 200 small dried beans as scoring tokens.

A player takes either a set of pegs or one of circles, and rolls one die per turn. He may split the roll between two pieces. If a peg and circle land on the same space, both players score: the pegger (piece landing) gets two points and the player pegged gets one point. The game ends when one player reaches the end of the track with all his pieces, and the winner is the one with the most points at that time.

There are three kinds of special spaces: Double Score does just that for both players pegging on one; Oh Peg It means taking a card from the deck and following instructions; and Chastity Square means no pegging here!

The sexual allusions in this Summer of Love era game are pretty obvious throughout. Aside from the male-female pegging mechanism, the cards also include sayings like "I could have pegged all night", "We'd rather peg than fight", and "Pegging makes the heart grow fonder". A second deck of blank cards is provided for those who want to get more daring or creative.

Description from the publisher:

Legendary Encounters conversion kit allows users to play Legendary Encounters Alien and Legendary Encounters Predator together.

This is an unusual spin-and-move game aimed at children between 5 and 9, and suggested for parties. It includes a spinner for each of four players and a set of 30 irregular cardboard shapes (five each of six colors). Instead of a board, players lay out their track ahead of time by placing the shapes like stepping stones across floors or lawns.

Players then spin as in a board game and act as the playing pieces themselves, stepping from one to the next. The rules suggest placing bonuses like lollipops for the first person landing on a step, and also say the path can be made easier or more difficult to suit the players.

This gorgeous 1940s game from All-Metal Products Co. is indeed made entirely of metal in the form of a hollow four-inch diameter apple. A metal post is mounted on the bottom of the apple inside, and a metal platter sits on top of the post.

The platter is decorated with pictures of six different fruits: apples, bananas, grapes, pears, cherries and plums. The fruit segments are numbered 1 to 6 as well, and each segment has a small indentation near the center. There is a set of 12 cardboard tokens, two of each fruit.

Players take turns dropping a small green marble onto the spinning platter and seeing where it comes to rest. They claim a corresponding token if still available. The winner is the first to accumulate an agreed number of tokens or number of points on the tokens.

Description from the publisher:

In Super Fighting Robot, you Punch, Block, Counterattack, Pulse, Energize, and Repair your way through the grueling gauntlet that is the Super Fighting Robot Championship! 
Defeat 4 robots plus the final boss challenge to claim supreme victory! The game comes with 8 different robots.

Choose a robot, add their specialized moves cards into the deck, and then battle by drawing and assigning moves 1 at a time to the 2x4 Ring. Cards in slots 1-4 are your opponent's moves and cards in slots 5-8 are yours! Spend energy on power ups to help deliver the final blow, but watch out, energy is in limited supply!

Super Fighting Robot is a solo game designed for the 2016 Solitaire Game Contest.

User summary

This second expansion set for the international version of Tragedy Looper contains one new Basic Tragedy Script, and nine scripts that take on two new Tragedy Sets: Prime Evil and Cosmic Mythology. In Prime Evil, experience a classic horror movie setting, chased by werewolves and vampires, while in Cosmic Mythology, gain access to ancient magic left over from an ancient time. It also contains four new characters: Teacher, Soldier, New Student and Cat, and variant versions of the characters Police Officer and Informer.

The two tragedy sets in this expansion are equivalent to the japanese original two expansions Haunted Stage Again and Weird Mythology. However, the original "Haunted Stage" from 2012 was for this re-release much re-vamped, and saw many changes of plots, roles and incidents.

Description from the publisher:

The 2016 Republican Primary was one of the most polarizing and exciting events to happen in American Politics in the last decade. The wildly different personalities of the candidates suddenly transformed the nomination process into a civil war of fundamental differences. In GOP Nominee, you will take on the role of one of the four major candidates running for the Republican Nomination.

GOP Nominee is an asymmetrical game played over 6 Rounds where each round represents 1 Month of the Primary Elections (Including Super Tuesday which counts as its own Round). A 7th Round is possible if the game goes to a Brokered Convention.

In each Round, Support Cubes may be gained or lost to Candidates and Actions will be played in an effort to increase the probability of your Support Cubes being drawn during the Voting Phase of the Round. However, Mud Cubes will also be gained by Candidates, decreasing their number of Support Cubes added to the drawing. Voting results determine which Candidates gain Delegates and get that much closer to winning the game.

Description from the publisher:

Galactic Traders is an easy to learn game of trading goods across the galaxy.
The game is played over several rounds divided in 2 phases:
1) Trade goods phase
2) Deployment phase
During the 1st phase, players scour the galaxy in search of trade goods they will later sell to planets in distant solar systems. Acquiring trade goods is very quick and simple. Players
draft cards from a deck of goods and add them to their hands. The first player draws cards equal to twice the number of players (for example, 6 cards for a 3 player game) chooses one and passes the rest to the next player. The rest of the players do the same, except for the last. The last player, chooses 2 cards then passes the rest to the previous player. This happens until the first player takes the last remaining card. (example: Nick, John and Mary have 6 cards to draft. Nick goes first chooses 1, then passes 5 cards to John. John also chooses 1, then passes 4 cards to Mary. Mary chooses 2 then passes 2 cards to John. John chooses 1 then passes the last card to Nick.)

During the 2nd phase, players deploy their trade goods gathered from the 1st phase on planets of their choice. But the real reward is not money. The real reward is the trade agreement that may be established with a planet. If such a trade agreement is active, a player may benefit from it later on the game.
Planets are divided in Age I and Age II. Age I planets provide trade agreements and just a few credits, while Age II planets provide lots of credits but no trade agreements.

Raptor Run 3D Game by Buffalo Games is a 3-dimensional game that builds right out of the box and then folds down neatly for storage when the game is over. Players race their raptors to the top of Dino Mountain rescuing baby dinosaur eggs along the way. But they have to be careful not to get knocked off by rolling boulders or they will be sent back down the mountain. The first player to collect an egg and carry it to the nest at the top of the mountain wins! Recommended for 2 to 4 players, ages 4+. Contents include 4 raptors, 10 raptor eggs, 1 boulder die, 5 raptor nests and a 10-inch tall 3-dimensional game board.
