The Checkmate Expansion introduces a king and queens into 3DPawn, bringing new possibilities and new strategies to explore.

Each Anno Domini game consists of 340 cards, with a description of a historical event on one side of the card and the year (and sometimes specific date) in which it happened on the other. Anno Domini: Gesundheit contains historical events related to health, medicine, etc. Like all Anno Domini sets, this game can be played as a standalone or mixed with some or even all editions.

In Anno Domini: Gesundheit, each player receives nine cards (or fewer, if you want the game to be shorter) and may look only at the descriptions. In turn, players place a card on the table, trying to place their card in chronological order to those already present. Instead of adding a card, a player may claim that the order in which the cards have been placed is incorrect. In this case all cards are turned over and the correct years revealed.

If the order is correct, then the doubting player receives two cards and skips a turn. If the order is incorrect, then the previous player – who accepted the order as correct or made it incorrect through her own placement – receives three cards. The first player with no cards remaining in hand wins.

Note that Anno Domini: Gesundheit & Ernährung was discontinued and split into Anno Domini: Gesundheit and Anno Domini: Essen & Trinken. About half of the events in each of these new editions were previously part of Anno Domini: Gesundheit & Ernährung and half of the events are new.

boardgame: Holy Roman Empire Expansion 1: Battles of the Thirty Years War
description:
Description from the publisher:

Holy Roman Empire is a strategic game, and while conflict is normally resolved abstractly by the throw of some dice, an Optional Tactical Combat system is included for those players seeking to add a bit more flavor to the game. This game-within-the-game system can also be used to re-fight some of the great battles of the Thirty Years War. The 12 scenarios included in this pack are not in-depth simulations of those epic contests; they are instead a collection of quick mini-games that are meant to allow players a glimpse into the way battles of that era were fought. As in any war, not every battle is an even contest; some are puzzles where players take turns commanding each army to see which of them can do better as the disadvantaged side--or through luck and skill turn an historic defeat into a surprising victory.

The 12 battles in this pack are presented in chronological order, and if fought in that order will give players a brief running history of the war. Contestants may choose to play the games in any order, may select favorite battles to re-fight or may make a campaign by taking each battle in turn, with one player always taking the Imperial side in each contest and then tallying the results of each battle to reach a final score of wins and losses.

Players resolve the battles in this booklet utilizing the Optional Tactical Combat systems as found in the Holy Roman Empire game. Additional options for "Create Your Own" Battle scenarios are also included in those rules.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
"Who are we? Where are We from? Explore the origins of humanity in this set collection game."

Aurora is a card game where players are creating star systems and trying reach sentience. Other victory conditions include maxing out resources (water, oxygen, carbon) and supporting life in multiple star systems.

Each turn players create, or expand, star systems, collect resources, and try to stall the expansion of other players' systems. Each turn you can play 1 negative action on another player and 2 actions on themselves. After cards have been played and resolved, resources are collected and unused cards are passed to the player on the left. Everyone then draws back up to 8 cards and a new round begins.

Aurora was a submission to The Game Crafter's Learning Games content.

Description from the publisher:

Torpedo Raiders is a solitaire WW2 air combat card game, depicting torpedo bombers and their exploits in historical scenarios.  The game system is similar to the game system used in Stringbag, which appeared in Panzer Digest #13.  Players take the role of one (or a group) of torpedo bombers and fly through flak to deliver torpedoes against enemy warships.

Includes eight-page illustrated rule booklet, two identical Reference Cards, and one counter sheet with individually rated warplanes. (Counters must be cut apart prior to play.)  Optional rules are provided as well. There are five historical scenarios that come with the game:  Taranto 1940 (involving British Fairey Swordfish planes), Bismarck 1941 (Swordfish), Pearl Harbor 1941 (with Japanese B5N Kates), Force Z 1941 (Japanese G3M Nells and G4M Bettys), and Midway 1942 (American Devastators).

This is a small solitaire game, that plays quickly, so it is ideal for short play sessions, overnight business trips, or tray table action while flying. All you need to do is supply a regular deck of cards, a six-sided die, the supplied plane counters and Reference Cards, and an imagination, and you are in business. Like other Minden designs, Torpedo Raiders delivers high playability and historicity for you to enjoy.

World War II torpedo bombing attacks create an exciting narrative for a wargame. The theater of your imagination--along with the straightforward mechanics of this game system--will bring these raids to life on your tabletop.

description:
Stacked! is a quick, co-op game set in a mystical vanishing library. Players must answer customer questions (using IRL library digital resources) while making sure the library doesn't fill up with unshelved books.

It was created as a tool to use during digital library workshops at Auckland Libraries.

Summary
 Stacked! is a cooperative game in which the players fight against the inevitable build-up of unshelved books while trying to answer as many customer questions as possible
 Stacked! is a magical vanishing library and, as such, the layout changes from day to day. During the game you're going to explore the library; answer reference questions from the customers; and prevent the returned books from piling up.
 In a turn, players have a choice of x actions. Move, Explore, Shelve (books), take a reference question, or use their special ability. If a player has a reference question they must attempt to find an answer between turns.

Winning the Game
 The players win or lose together. The players win when each player has at least 2 answered questions.
 The players lose if the Library is too full of unshelved books (I.e. Trolley Tokens are all gone).

Stacked! can be played using your local library's digital resources (i.e. as a training tool etc) or using google to find the answers.

Description from the publisher:

It’s dark. The streets are quiet, and all the grown-ups are at home. However deep in the city, inside a small arcade parlor, furious battle rages on. Amidst the dazzling flashes and booming beep-and-bop of the arcade machines, Champions battle each other for pride, honor, and extra lives. Joysticks are their swords and buttons their shields; their amazing game playing skill are the stuff of legend. One wrong move, and it is Game Over, man. Who will win the crown of Arcade King? Take the challenge and join the game!

Battle at the Arcade is a press-your-luck card game. A cross elements of a dice game and poker (but play without dice). Players are dealt cards from a deck, and must match them to the level’s requirement in order to earn points. However, players may also perform “Special Tricks”, swap cards and points, in order to “beat the level”. More than just luck, a deep strategic layer of choice and decision are needed to win the game. What are you waiting for, lets get the GAME ON!

FEATURES .............................................


     Easy to set up, just shuffle and go. 
     Champion, Arcade, and Score cards are printed on Jumbo Cards, to make them easier to read. 
     10 different "Champions of the Arcade" to pick from. Each with their unique combination of special abilities, strength, and weakness.
     Relive the glory of the golden age Arcades. 10 different Arcade games to play from, inspired by the Classics, that adds more variation to each game.
     Chain your move together to score BIG. Flashy combos could be made to chain one move to another. 
     Easy to use Score cards, with color matching cubes, to help you keep track of your game stats.
     Supports up to 4 players (more players if don’t mind using other paper to track the score. Or get another copy *smile* ).
     Full of "OH YEAH!" and "Ah, COOOOME ON" Moments.

Description from the publisher:
A Game of Yum Yum Dessert Grabbing and Sweet Sweet Deduction
Oh NO! The Mistress of the house is gone, and all the lights just went out! But… the door to the dessert closet was left wide open! All the yum-yum goodies are for the taken! Under the cover of darkness, the household pets are scrambling to grab their favorite desserts. But who are those rascals? It’s up to the famous De-cat-ive Furlock Holems to uncover their identities!

Cookie Mookie is a set-collection micro-game with element of mystery for 2-4 players. Players are assigned a character role, with the goal to get the highest points to win the game. However, players are encouraged to keep their goal a secret, else they can be worked against you. The game is split into 4 short phases, the Setup, the Grab, the Swap, and the Deduction.

Easy to learn, quick to play. A great game for the whole family, or the sweet lovers!

				
				
					=======A short description of the game-play:

First desert cards are dealt out on the able in a grid. Each player are handed a character role. Each role have particular type of desserts that they love, which would score them bigger points, as well ones they dislike, which give the negative points. Each player then takes turn plucking cards from the grid. After each player have a certain number of cards, they then swap one card with their adjacent players. If there is detective character in game, they get to deduce other player’s role. Else the turn ends, and the score are counted up to see who is the winner.

The Strategy comes at card picking phase. Not only do you want to be mindful of what other are pick, but also what you pick, so to get the max points, but without giving yourself away. This allow you fool other during the card swapping, so go give you a card that would better benefit you, while give them a card that might reduce their score.

You play the role of a special operative, as you wage a one-person war against “The Organization”. Blaze your way through levels of mercenaries, henchmen and terrorists to save humanity from certain destruction. Guns, bullets, and the sweet yet salty taste of victory await you.


     Designed primary for solo play, allowing you to easily get a quick game going by just yourself.

     60 unique graphic designed cards to immerse you in the intense gun battles and the dangerous hazards you would face as a solo infiltrator.



     Just as important as luck, a good strategic decision-making and resource management are a must to win the game. 



     Easy setup, minimal mess. Just separate the cards into 3 decks, and you are ready to go. 



     Supports additional game mode that allows 2 players competitive play.

This is a compilation of 4 skiing games.

1. Der Spezialsprunglauf (Ski jumping)

Each player has 3 jumps and the 2 best results count to the end result. First the player rolls the 3 white dice to determine the length of the jump. If one die shows a "0" the jumper has fallen. Then the 3 black dice determine the posture grade. The Result of each jump is marked on the game board by plaques with the starting numbers of each player.

2. Langlauf (cross country skiing)

The players take turns to roll the 2 white dice with the single digits and move their figures. The first player to reach the finish gets 240 points, the second gets 235 points. Each arriving player gets 5 points less than the one before.

For the nordic combination, the results from the two first games are combined to determine a winner.

3. Der Abfahrtslauf (downhill skiing)

On the square grid route is marked with 20 red flags which have a distance of 5 squares from each other. The players roll the 2 white dice with the single digits and move their figures. The players have to pass each flag on an adjacent space.

The second player starts after the first one has rolled and moved three times and so on. The player who completes the route with the fewest rolls wins the game.

4. Der Torlauf (Slalom)

This is played like game 3, but the flags are placed as gates in a slalom track.

The back of the rules includes the number sequences "111/9/105", "144" and "0742/1/73", which may or may not indicate the time of publication and/or a product number.

It puts you in the captains seat of a Hammerhead class cargo vessel, tasked with transporting goods through pirate infested space to a destination planet. You must choose your crew wisely to achieve this goal, then effectively command them throughout the voyage to ensure your cargo is intact when you finally dock. You will issue firing orders, request scans, change power priorities, assign repairs, adjust speed and you can even take a detour to investigate areas of interest. All the while, pirates will be attacking you in an attempt to knock out your engines, shields and weapons, thereby allowing them to board your ship and loot the cargo.

At the end of the mission, you will be paid for any cargo you have remaining, but you will also have an bonuses and expenses to factor into your final pay - bonuses consist of bounties for pirates destroyed, or an early arrival bonus if you manage to get there before your ETA - expenses include fines for any neutral targets you may have killed, and repair costs which can be astronomical if your ship is too badly beaten up.

description:
A bundle of mini-expansions that were available during the Kickstarter for The Pursuit of Happiness.

The Events mini-expansion adds special events that are drawn at random at the beginning of every adult round and come into effect at the beginning at the next round (4 in total). The deck contains both positive and negative events and the player is able ensure that they get the positive effect of a card or prevent that they get a negative one by performing the task on the card during the round as it is not activated till the beginning of the following round. Events include such positive events as "Technological Breakthrough" that allow you pay less resources for Projects, negative events such as "Financial Crisis" that make Projects more expensive and even "Natural Disaster" that cannot be prevented and causes a loss of Short Term Happiness.

The Trends mini-expansion adds special trends that are drawn at the beginning of the game and effects play throughout. Three are drawn at random and illustrate which categories will be popular through the players lifetime. At the end of the game the players will gain additional Long Time Happiness by having completed projects in these categories.

The Pets mini-expansion adds pets to the Item-Activity cards. Pets are different in that you do not have to use card actions to advance them - they do this by themselves during upkeep as long as you feed them (pay the upkeep cost).

The Stand-Alone Jobs mini-expansion consists of 3 new jobs that are shuffled in with the standard job cards. The difference between standard and stand-alone jobs are that with a stand-alone jobs you can not be promoted. Instead you can choose to work harder. This is done by paying up to two times the cost in the Work Harder section and thereby getting the same times the reward. This applies to the upkeep phase as well.

User summary:
浄土双六 (Joudo-Sugoroku) is a traditional Japanese game originally produced during the Muromachi period (1336 to 1573) and later developed into Tobi-Sugoroku in the Edo Period (1603 to 1868). As with the later Tobi-Sugoroku (and Mawari-Sugoroku), the original designers' and illustrators' names are lost to history.

Joudo-Sugoroku is themed on the Buddhist tradition of re-incarnation. Joudo means 'The Pure Land', that is the celestial realm or pure abode of a Buddha.  The players are human souls, wandering through the many Hells, gradually gaining enlightenment through training and meditation, to finally ascend to the Pure Land.

Game-play is based upon the text in the squares on the scroll. The players roll dice (labelled in Chinese text for the numbers 1 to 6) and move as instructed, so a player does not move from one square to an adjacent one, but instead jumps across the network. (Sugoroku means literally 'double-six').

An edition has been produced by 望月哲史 (Satoshi Mochizuki) for Plenluno (プレンルーノ) and uses the classic artwork and language of the "Sugoroku Ruiju" game held in the collection of the National Museum of Japan in Tokyo, but also updated into modern language. The game is produced as a large sheet poster, with additional playing pieces, and is available at Spiel 2016.

The game is played by 1 to 8 players, aged from 5+ years and takes around 20 minutes to finish. It's a roll-and-move racing game using dice, with mythological and educational themes.

Game description from the publisher:
江戸時代に遊ばれていた「飛び双六」の現代語訳版です。
日本の東京国立博物館に収蔵されている「双六類聚」を現代語に翻訳しました。
人間界から出発し、地獄を巡り、修業をして悟りを開き、仏になることを目指す双六です。
江戸時代、お寺などで遊ばれていた双六です。
400年以上前のものの復刻版です。
盤面は仏教の死後の世界を表しています。
地獄をさまよったり、修行や瞑想をしたりして、悟りを開いて仏を目指します。
輪廻転生を繰り返し、仏を目指しましょう。

User summary
Tobi-Sugoroku is a traditional Japanese game developed from the original game Joudo-Sugoroku. Tobi means 'Jumping', and as in Joudo-Sugoroku, players move differently across the board using dice rolls and following the instructions on the squares. As with other classical Japanese games, the original designers' and illustrators are not known.

However, later Tobi-Sugoroku do not keep the Buddhist theme, and instead reflect various popular subjects (New Year, ghosts and monsters, warriors and heroes, morality). This allowed the publishers to produce beautifully illustrated prints in the Edo period, and 19C and 20C magazines to have Sugoroku games as cheap pull-outs.

The main difference between Tobi-Sugoroku and Mawari-Sugoroku is that in the former, movement is across the network of squares, whereas in the latter, movement is along a controlled path (similar to Game of Goose). Any Sugoroku game with pictures is known as E-Sugoroku (E- means 'picture').

Keymelequin is a mini-expansion for the game Keyflower that consists of the two colored Keymelequin keyples — Paulo and Sven — and a ship tile, the Keymelequin.

When the Keymelequin arrives in port, it transports two new characters, Paulo and Sven. Sven, who is a translator, co-ordinator, and peacemaker between the blue and yellow keyples, disembarks from the Keymelequin at the end of spring. Paulo, who has similar abilities with red and green keyples, arrives on the Keymelequin at the end of the summer. Paulo and Sven will facilitate teamwork and cooperation between their respective factions, with powerful and interesting results.

Keymelequin is playable only with a copy of the game Keyflower, and it can be used with the base game on its own or in conjunction with any combination of the other Keyflower promotional tiles and expansions.
Lightning is a fast reflex game that uses creatures from the Chinese zodiac. Players win by playing all of their cards before anyone else, with everyone playing cards simultaneously.

description:
Description from the designer:

The Racket Wars game can be played with 2 – 6 players.

Description from the publisher:

The AI's at the abandoned spaceport got bored.  To pass the days they used the maintenance systems to modify two of the small housekeeping bots to race around the site's reconfigurable service-ways. As they are semi autonomous the 'bots have to receive their directions in burst transmissions.  Now the AI's are happy steering, drifting, and jumping their machines against each other in full on races.

In Microbot Racers players assume the role of one of the two spaceport AI's racing the other.  Each round they select the actions their 'bot can take on the action card before sending them their instructions in a burst transmission.  Players will need to avoid obstacles, hazards and each other in their mad dash round the track, but can make life difficult for one another by triggering the reconfiguration switches that cause sections of track to rotate or flip, changing the course in mid-race.

This game was an entry in the 2016 2 player PnP competition.  A microgame requiring only the 11 cards and a few extra components, its is quick and easy to build and play.

Dicedoku is a small game you can even print it on business card.
Each player take a sheet with 6x6 grid.
Each turn,roll a die to decide a tetris shape and roll 4 dice to decide 4 numbers(if 3 or more dice are same number,reroll them).Each player then need to fill in their 6x6 grid using these 4 numbers and this shape.Shape can be rotated or flipped.Shape can overlap on exist numbers or outside of 6x6 grid,then these overlap or outside positions dont need to be write number(old number stay).
The object is,like Sudoku, make as many rows,columns and 3x2 blocks contain all digits of 1~6 as possible.
First player complete a row, a column or a block contain 1~6 will get 3 points of bonus.
At the end of game, each complete rows, columns and blocks give player 3 points.
Each empty space is -1 point.
Player with most points wins!

User summary
迷家-マヨイガ - The Lost Village (MAYOIGA - The Lost Village) is a game from a popular Japanese anime series. Mayoiga translates as "Illusion House" and the name comes from Japanese folklore stories which say that those who have visited the mysterious house in a hidden village will gain wealth. In the anime, there is a hidden village but no illusion house.

Form a team of five out of 30 characters, and survive them!

"Mayoiga - The Lost Village" is a survival horror card game which is based on the Japanese TV anime program of the same name. In this game, characters confront the NANAKI, monstrous beings shaped by the characters' own mental traumas.

Reveal a number of character cards equal to the number of players plus one on the table. Players strengthen the characters' mind by playing mind cards of five types on them. Then each player adds one of the characters to that player's team, along with mind cards on that character. However, the NANAKI devour the remaining cards on the table, making themselves hard to be overcome. At the end, the characters must confront the NANAKI which has been grown by the players' intentions!

description:
User summary
爆弾宝箱 (Bakudan Takarabako), in English is Bomb & Treasure.

In the game of Bomb & Treasure, players are pirates who try to grab treasures while avoiding bombs.

Each round, a player (sender) places a card face-down, which is either a treasure of 1-10 value or a bomb, and sends it to another player (receiver), saying the value the card. The sender may lie if it is a bomb and say any value, but if it is a number card, they cannot lie and must say the actual value of the card. The receiver may either accept and reveal it, or sends it back to the sender and forces the sender to reveal it, or adds another card and passes both cards to any other player, including the sender.

Now the card or cards must be revealed and the player who has them takes all of them, including bombs revealed, and puts them face-up in front of themself. After each player has taken a turn as sender, players draw back to five cards in hand.

With total of three bombs, that player is out of the game. If a player has two bombs in front of them, they don't want to risk getting a third bomb, and so may easily decline an offer of a high value card.

If a player gets a set of 1,2 & 3, they equal 10 points, so players may accept low value cards and be fooled into taking a bomb.

You win the game if you accumulate 30 value of treasures, or all other players are out of the game, or you have the highest value of treasures when the ninth bomb is revealed.

Publisher's summary
プレイヤーは海賊となって、宝物と爆弾を送りあいます。うまく宝物だけをゲットし、爆弾を相手に押し付けましょう！
手札のカードを1枚伏せて、他の人の前に出します。
カードを出された人は、「受け取る」「返す」「さらに1枚追加して他の人に渡す」のどれかを選びます。
宝物であれば点数に、爆弾であればダメージを受けます。爆弾を3枚引き取ってしまったら脱落です。
「先に30点以上集める」「自分以外の全員が脱落する」「9枚爆弾が出た時に、一番多くの点数を持っている」のどれかで勝ちになります。

Description from the publisher:

What is time? Is it like a river? Or maybe an ocean? Is it like up, but sideways? Is it churning chaos, background noise, held together only briefly by our own awareness of it? You don't know. You just work the machinery; someone else built it. To you, time is a means to an end, a glorious end in which humanity's crowning achievement turns out to be your own benevolent rule. It's a simple process of weeding through the alternatives, snipping prudently — an ungrateful utopia here, a useless revolution there. In the end, from the Age of Atlantis to the Zombie Apocalypse, the eras will sing your praise.

Temporum: Alternate Realities, an expansion for Temporum, adds 48 more Zones and 60 more Player cards, plus chits and cards used by the new Zones.

Topoum: Bratatatata – Expansion Deck I consists of:


     49 cards in 7 new types
     1 farmer card
     1 Event card
     4 player aids card
     1 rulebook

Description from the designer:

Players take turns to swap a card, then predict where an arrow will travel to. It's critical to not only remember what the face-down cards are, but also consider what you're swapping, what cards you're giving to the other player, and what they could swap.

Setup: make a 3x3 grid of cards. Both players see the middle 3. Each player looks at the 3 closest to themselves. The remaining 3 cards are given to the start player, along with the arrow.

Each turn:

     the arrow is placed in front of the central card on the active player's side.
     the active player swaps a card on the table with one from their hand, then gives the 3 cards to the other player.
     the other player swaps a different card on the table.
     the active player predicts which side of the grid the arrow will exit on
     the other player chooses a different side
     cards are turned over, one at a time, to reveal the path that the arrow follows, until it exits the grid
     if either player predicted the exit side correctly, they get a point.


First to 3 points wins the game.

Overview:
Your city is being threatened by ruthless and corrupt organizations who seek to profit off the backs of your friends and neighbors. It’s time for someone to stand up and fight back. The odds are stacked against you and your fellow justice seekers, but maybe, with the right strategy, equipment and training you can chase the Minions out of your city.

Objective:
Create a hero and go on patrol to take down the corrupt organizations and their minions that threaten your city. Beat the clock, against schemes that empower the Villain, uncovering their identity and stopping them once and for all.

Gameplay:
Vigilante is a campaign, deck construction game with a sprawling city of Schemes that you and your friends' heroes move through, playing cards and rolling dice to combat. The challenge is knowing which Schemes are most important to stop, because the ones that you fail to prevent add new abilities to the Villain and his lair. Reward cards will give you temporary upgrades and gear can be combined to unlock new equipment and weapons. Upgrade your team's base or your own vehicle? The choice is yours!
